title: Entity Classification and Selection
description: Understand the different business entity types and their tax implications

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Entity selection is one of the most important decisions a business makes! The choice affects taxes, liability, operations, and exit strategies. As an EA, you'll help clients evaluate and sometimes change their entity structure.

sections.title: Sole Proprietorship
sections.content:
**Simplest form - you ARE the business**

**Characteristics:**
• No separate entity for tax purposes
• Report income/expenses on Schedule C
• Owner is personally liable for all debts
• Self-employment tax on net profit

**Formed:** Automatically when you start doing business alone

**Common for:** Freelancers, consultants, small retailers

sections.title: Partnership
sections.content:
**Two+ persons carrying on business as co-owners**

**Types:**
• General Partnership (GP) - all partners have management rights and liability
• Limited Partnership (LP) - at least one GP, limited partners have limited liability
• Limited Liability Partnership (LLP) - professional services, protects from other partners' malpractice

**Tax Treatment:**
• Entity files Form 1065 (informational)
• Income/loss passes through to partners via K-1
• Partners pay tax on their share, even if not distributed
• No entity-level tax (pass-through)

sections.title: S Corporation
sections.content:
**Corporation that elects pass-through taxation**

**Requirements (Eligibility Rules):**
• Domestic corporation
• ≤100 shareholders (family members count as one)
• Only one class of stock (voting differences allowed)
• Shareholders must be individuals, estates, certain trusts
• No nonresident alien shareholders
• Not a bank, insurance company, or DISC

**File Form 2553** to elect S status (by March 15 for calendar year)

**Advantages:**
• Pass-through taxation (no double tax)
• Limit SE tax to reasonable compensation
• Liability protection

sections.title: C Corporation
sections.content:
**Traditional corporation with entity-level taxation**

**Characteristics:**
• Separate legal and tax entity
• Corporate tax rate: 21% (flat rate)
• Double taxation: corporate tax + dividend tax
• Unlimited shareholders, any stock structure

**When C Corp makes sense:**
• Planning to go public
• Need flexible stock structures
• Reinvesting profits (21% rate lower than individual rates)
• Qualified small business stock exclusion (Section 1202)

sections.title: LLC Tax Classification
sections.content:
**LLCs are "check-the-box" entities**

**Default Classification:**
• Single-member LLC = Disregarded (sole proprietorship)
• Multi-member LLC = Partnership

**Can Elect:**
• Corporation (then S election if desired)
• Must file Form 8832 to change classification

**Key:** LLC is a state law concept; IRS looks at tax classification!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Entity Selection
sections.content:
**"SPICE"** factors for entity choice:

**S**elf-employment tax minimization
**P**ass-through vs. entity-level taxation
**I**nvestors and ownership structure needs
**C**omplexity tolerance (filings, formalities)
**E**xit strategy (sale, IPO, succession)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sole proprietorship = simplest, but no liability protection
Partnership = pass-through for 2+ owners, report on Form 1065
S corporation = pass-through with liability protection, strict rules
C corporation = double taxation but maximum flexibility
LLC is a state law creature - tax classification is separate
Form 8832 changes LLC tax classification
Form 2553 elects S corporation status

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following entity types is NOT eligible to elect S corporation status?
options: A domestic corporation with 90 shareholders, all of whom are U.S. citizens.
A domestic corporation with 105 shareholders, where 6 are nonresident aliens.
A domestic corporation with 95 shareholders, all of whom are U.S. citizens and residents.
A domestic corporation with 100 shareholders, all of whom are U.S. citizens and residents.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: S corporations are not permitted to have nonresident alien shareholders or more than 100 shareholders. Therefore, a corporation with nonresident alien shareholders is ineligible to elect S corporation status.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the eligibility requirements related to the number and types of shareholders.

reveal.question: What tax form does a sole proprietor use to report business income and expenses?
reveal.answer: Schedule C (Form 1040), Profit or Loss From Business (Sole Proprietorship)

rows.label: Liability
values: All partners have unlimited liability for partnership debts.
General partners have unlimited liability; limited partners have limited liability.
Partners are generally not liable for the negligence or malpractice of other partners.

rows.label: Management
values: All partners typically have management rights.
General partners typically manage the business; limited partners have limited management rights.
All partners typically have management rights.

title: Schedule C: Reporting Business Income
description: Master the proper reporting of sole proprietorship income and expenses

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Schedule C is one of the most common business forms you'll prepare. Understanding what income to report, what expenses are deductible, and how to calculate net profit is essential for every tax practitioner.

sections.title: Schedule C Structure
sections.content:
**Part I - Income:**
• Gross receipts or sales
• Returns and allowances
• Cost of goods sold
• = Gross profit
• + Other income
• = Gross income

**Part II - Expenses:**
• Advertising, car expenses, depreciation
• Insurance, interest, legal/professional
• Rent, repairs, supplies, utilities, wages
• Other expenses (attach explanation)

**Bottom Line:**
Gross income - Total expenses = Net profit (or loss)

sections.title: Gross Receipts
sections.content:
**Include ALL income from business:**

• Cash received
• Checks received
• Credit card payments
• Bartering (fair market value)
• Cancelled debt (if business debt)
• Kickbacks and rebates

**Report when constructively received:**
If amount is available to you, it's income - even if you haven't deposited it yet.

sections.title: Cost of Goods Sold
sections.content:
**For businesses that sell products:**

**Calculation:**
Beginning inventory
+ Purchases
+ Labor costs (direct)
+ Materials and supplies
+ Other costs
= Cost of goods available for sale
- Ending inventory
= **Cost of Goods Sold**

**Inventory methods:**
• FIFO (First-In, First-Out)
• LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) - election required
• Average cost
• Specific identification

sections.title: Home Office Deduction
sections.content:
**Two methods:**

**1. Simplified Method:**
• $5 per square foot
• Maximum 300 sq ft
• Maximum deduction: $1,500
• No depreciation, no carryover

**2. Regular Method:**
• Calculate business % of home (sq ft or rooms)
• Actual expenses × business %
• Include mortgage interest, taxes, insurance, utilities, depreciation
• Can create home office carryover

**Exclusive and Regular Use Required:**
Area must be used exclusively and regularly for business (daycare and storage have exceptions).

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Self-Employment Tax
sections.content:
**Net Profit from Schedule C is Subject to Self-Employment Tax!**

• Social Security: 12.4% on first $168,600 (2024)
• Medicare: 2.9% on all earnings
• Additional Medicare: 0.9% over $200K ($250K MFJ)

**Deductible:** 50% of SE tax is above-the-line deduction

**Key:** This is separate from income tax - clients often forget about SE tax!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Report all gross receipts when constructively received
COGS available for businesses selling products
Expenses must be ordinary and necessary
Home office: simplified ($5/sq ft max $1,500) or regular method
Net profit subject to self-employment tax (15.3%)
50% of SE tax is deductible above-the-line
Standard mileage rate 67¢/mile for 2024

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah owns a bakery. In 2023, she received $50,000 in cash, $20,000 in checks, and $10,000 in credit card payments for her goods. She also bartered $500 worth of cupcakes for accounting services and had $1,000 of business debt forgiven. However, a customer wrote a bad check for $100 in December but Sarah didn't discover it until January of the next year. What is Sarah's gross receipts for Schedule C in 2023?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Gross receipts include all income received from the business, including cash, checks, credit card payments, bartering (at fair market value), and cancelled business debt. The bad check is not included in 2023 since it was not discovered until the following year. $50,000 (cash) + $20,000 (checks) + $10,000 (credit cards) + $500 (bartering) + $1,000 (debt forgiveness) = $81,500.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include all forms of income, including non-cash transactions like bartering and debt forgiveness.

reveal.question: State the formula for calculating Cost of Goods Sold (COGS).
reveal.answer: Beginning Inventory + Purchases + Direct Labor Costs + Materials and Supplies + Other Costs = Cost of Goods Available for Sale. Then, Cost of Goods Available for Sale - Ending Inventory = Cost of Goods Sold.

knowledgeCheck.question: John runs a small woodworking business. His beginning inventory was $5,000. He purchased $2,000 in lumber and $500 in hardware. He paid $3,000 in wages to his assistant who helps assemble the furniture. Other costs totaled $200. His ending inventory was valued at $4,000. What is John's Cost of Goods Sold?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Beginning Inventory ($5,000) + Purchases ($2,000 + $500) + Labor Costs ($3,000) + Other Costs ($200) = $10,700 (Cost of Goods Available for Sale). $10,700 - Ending Inventory ($4,000) = $6,700.  Thus, John's COGS is $6,700.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Carefully consider all the elements that are part of purchases and apply the COGS formula.

title: Accounting Methods and Tax Years
description: Understand cash vs. accrual accounting and permitted tax years

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Accounting method determines WHEN income and expenses are recognized. Tax year determines the 12-month period for reporting. Getting these wrong can cause major problems with the IRS.

sections.title: Cash Method Details
sections.content:
**Income Recognition:**
• When actually or constructively received
• Constructive receipt = available to you without restriction
• Checks count when received, not when deposited

**Expense Deduction:**
• When paid (check mailed counts as paid)
• Credit card charges deductible when charged
• Prepaid expenses: may require capitalization if > 12 months

**Who Must Use Accrual:**
• C corporations with > $30 million gross receipts (3-year avg)
• Tax shelters
• Businesses with inventory (exceptions for small taxpayers)

sections.title: Accrual Method Details
sections.content:
**Income Recognition - All Events Test:**
• All events have occurred to fix the right to receive
• Amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy
• Economic performance has occurred (usually)

**Economic Performance:**
• Services/property provided TO you: as performed/provided
• Services/property provided BY you: as you perform/provide
• Use of property: as used

**Expense Deduction:**
• Must meet all events test AND economic performance
• Some exceptions for recurring items

sections.title: Tax Years
sections.content:
**Calendar Year:** January 1 - December 31
**Fiscal Year:** Any 12-consecutive-month period ending on last day of a month (not December)
**52-53 Week Year:** Ends same day of week closest to month end

**Who MUST use Calendar Year:**
• Individuals (unless proper books)
• S corporations (unless business purpose)
• Personal service corporations (unless business purpose)
• Partnerships with individual partners (matching requirement)

sections.title: Changing Accounting Method
sections.content:
**Generally requires IRS approval:**

**Form 3115:** Application for Change in Accounting Method

**Automatic approval** available for many changes

**Section 481(a) Adjustment:**
• Prevents double-counting or skipping income/deductions
• Positive adjustment (increased income): spread over 4 years
• Negative adjustment: take in year of change

**Must have specific permission to use method originally - can't just start using different method!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cash method: income when received, expenses when paid
Accrual method: income when earned, expenses when incurred
Most small businesses can use cash method
Large C corps (>$30M receipts) must use accrual
Calendar year is January 1 - December 31
Form 3115 required to change accounting method
Section 481(a) adjustment prevents duplication or omission

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a cash-basis taxpayer, performed services for a client on December 28th, Year 1, and mailed an invoice immediately. He received the client's check on January 3rd, Year 2. When is the income recognized?
options: December 28th, Year 1
December 31st, Year 1
January 3rd, Year 2
When the invoice was mailed
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under the cash method, income is recognized when actually or constructively received. John received the check (actual receipt) on January 3rd, Year 2. Therefore, the income is recognized in Year 2.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the core principle of the cash method: income when you have it in hand (or can get it without restriction).

reveal.question: Define a fiscal tax year.
reveal.answer: A fiscal tax year is any 12-consecutive-month period ending on the last day of a month other than December. For example, July 1st to June 30th.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corp, an accrual-basis taxpayer, contracted with XYZ Company for XYZ to provide consulting services. ABC Corp paid XYZ in December Year 1, but XYZ did not perform the services until February Year 2. When can ABC Corp deduct the expense?
options: December Year 1
February Year 2
Ratably over the period of the contract
When the contract was signed
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under the accrual method, an expense is deductible when all events have occurred to fix the liability, the amount can be determined with reasonable accuracy, AND economic performance has occurred. Economic performance for services provided TO you occurs as the services are performed. Therefore, ABC Corp can deduct the expense in February Year 2 when the services are performed.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Economic performance is a key component of the accrual method. Consider when the service or property is actually provided.

title: Business Deductions: General Rules
description: Master the requirements for deducting business expenses

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Not everything a business pays for is deductible! Knowing the difference between current deductions, capital expenditures, and non-deductible items is fundamental to tax practice.

sections.title: The Ordinary and Necessary Test
sections.content:
**Section 162: Trade or Business Expenses**

To be deductible, an expense must be:

**1. Ordinary:**
• Common and accepted in your type of business
• Not necessarily frequent - just normal for industry

**2. Necessary:**
• Helpful and appropriate for business
• Need not be indispensable

**3. Paid or incurred during the tax year**

**4. Connected to your trade or business**

**Key:** Both tests must be met!

sections.title: Start-Up Costs
sections.content:
**What are Start-Up Costs?**
Costs incurred before business begins that would be deductible if incurred in an existing business.

**Examples:**
• Market surveys
• Training employees
• Travel to secure suppliers/customers
• Professional fees (accounting, legal setup)

**Treatment:**
• Deduct first $5,000 in year business begins
• $5,000 reduced dollar-for-dollar if costs exceed $50,000
• Remainder amortized over 180 months

**Organizational Costs:** Same treatment for partnership/corporate formation costs.

sections.title: Non-Deductible Expenses
content: Federal income tax payments
Penalties and fines to government
Bribes and kickbacks (illegal payments)
Political contributions
Lobbying expenses (with exceptions)
Personal living expenses
Capital expenditures (unless depreciated/amortized)
Expenses not related to business

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Meals and Entertainment
sections.content:
**Current Rules (Post-TCJA):**

**Business Meals:** 50% deductible
• Must be directly related to business
• Not lavish or extravagant
• Taxpayer or employee present

**Entertainment:** 0% deductible
• Golf, concerts, sporting events = NOT deductible
• Even if business is discussed

**Exception:** Food/beverage provided separately from entertainment may still be 50% deductible.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Expenses must be ordinary AND necessary to be deductible
Capital expenditures must be depreciated/amortized
Start-up costs: $5,000 immediate, rest amortized 180 months
Federal taxes and penalties are not deductible
Business meals: 50% deductible
Entertainment expenses: NOT deductible (post-TCJA)
Personal expenses never deductible

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following expenses would MOST likely be considered 'necessary' for purposes of deducting a business expense under Section 162?
options: Extravagant office decorations to impress potential clients.
Payments to a consultant to improve the efficiency of the business's operations.
A contribution to a political campaign fund to influence legislation beneficial to the business.
Fines paid for violating environmental regulations.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While not indispensable, consulting services to improve efficiency are helpful and appropriate for the business. Extravagant decorations are unlikely to be considered necessary. Political contributions and fines are generally not deductible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which expense directly relates to improving the business's core operations.

reveal.question: What is the maximum amount of start-up costs that a taxpayer can elect to deduct in the first year of business?
reveal.answer: A taxpayer can deduct up to $5,000 of start-up costs in the first year. This amount is reduced dollar-for-dollar for start-up costs exceeding $50,000. Any remaining start-up costs are amortized over 180 months (15 years), beginning with the month the business begins operating.

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the BEST example of a start-up cost that could be eligible for the initial $5,000 deduction (subject to the phase-out rules)?
options: The purchase of a new delivery truck for the business.
Legal fees for drafting the business's operating agreement before the business began operations.
Repairs to the building the business will operate from.
Inventory purchased after the business opened its doors.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Legal fees incurred before the business begins operations, for services that would be deductible if the business were already operating, are considered start-up costs. The delivery truck is a capital expense. Repairs are typically deductible but not a start-up cost. Inventory is not a start-up cost.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on costs incurred BEFORE the business starts operating.

title: Depreciation Fundamentals
description: Understand MACRS depreciation methods and conventions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Depreciation is how businesses recover the cost of capital assets over time. MACRS rules determine how much deduction you get each year. This is heavily tested on the SEE!

sections.title: MACRS Methods
sections.content:
**Personal Property (3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20-year):**
• 200% declining balance (default for most)
• Switch to straight-line when beneficial

**Real Property:**
• Straight-line only
• 27.5 years for residential rental
• 39 years for nonresidential

**Alternative Depreciation System (ADS):**
• Straight-line over longer periods
• Required for some property (tax-exempt use, etc.)
• May elect for any property

sections.title: Depreciation Conventions
rows: Half-year depreciation in first and last year
If >40% placed in service in Q4
Depreciation based on quarter placed in service
Depreciation based on month placed in service

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Vehicle Depreciation Limits
sections.content:
**Luxury Auto Limits (2024):**

**Passenger Vehicles (not SUVs over 6,000 lbs GVW):**
• Year 1: $12,200 (or $20,200 with bonus)
• Year 2: $19,500
• Year 3: $11,700
• Year 4+: $6,960 per year

**SUVs/Trucks over 6,000 lbs:**
• No luxury limits (but watch listed property rules)
• Section 179 limited to $30,500

sections.title: Listed Property
sections.content:
**What is Listed Property:**
• Passenger vehicles
• Entertainment/recreation property
• Computers (unless used exclusively at regular business establishment)

**Special Requirements:**
• Must keep records of business/personal use
• Business use > 50% required for MACRS/Section 179
• If business use drops to ≤ 50%, must recapture excess depreciation

**Employees:** Must be for convenience of employer and required as condition of employment to claim depreciation.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: MACRS is the standard depreciation method
Computers and vehicles are 5-year property
Office furniture is 7-year property
Real property: 27.5 years residential, 39 years nonresidential
Half-year convention is default; mid-quarter if Q4 > 40%
Luxury auto limits cap depreciation on expensive vehicles
Listed property requires business use records

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements regarding the Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is CORRECT?
options: The 200% declining balance method is the default depreciation method for all types of property under MACRS.
Real property, such as a commercial building, is depreciated using the 150% declining balance method.
Residential rental property is depreciated using the straight-line method over a 39-year recovery period.
The Alternative Depreciation System (ADS) generally uses longer recovery periods than the general MACRS system.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Alternative Depreciation System (ADS) typically uses longer recovery periods than the general MACRS system. The 200% declining balance method is the default for most personal property, not all property. Real property uses straight-line. Residential rental property uses 27.5 years, not 39.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the recovery periods and acceptable methods for real versus personal property.

reveal.question: What is the recovery period for nonresidential real property under MACRS?
reveal.answer: 39 years

rows.label: Depreciation Method
values: 200% DB (personal property, default), 150% DB (certain property), Straight-line (real property)
Straight-line

rows.label: Recovery Periods
values: Shorter (e.g., 5 years for computers)
Longer (e.g., 12 years for computers)

rows.label: Used When
values: Generally used unless ADS is required or elected
Required in certain situations (e.g., tax-exempt use property) or may be elected.

title: Section 179 and Bonus Depreciation
description: Master the accelerated cost recovery methods

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Section 179 and bonus depreciation allow immediate deduction of asset costs! Understanding when to use each and the interplay between them is essential for tax planning.

sections.title: Section 179 Overview (2024)
sections.content:
**What is Section 179?**
An election to expense (deduct immediately) the cost of qualifying property instead of depreciating over time.

**2024 Limits:**
• Maximum deduction: $1,220,000
• Phase-out threshold: $3,050,000
• Completely phased out: $4,270,000

**The phase-out:**
Section 179 limit reduced dollar-for-dollar by amount of qualifying property placed in service over $3,050,000.

sections.title: Section 179 Qualifying Property
content: Tangible personal property (machinery, equipment)
Off-the-shelf computer software
Qualified improvement property (roof, HVAC, fire protection, security)
Certain real property (qualified retail, restaurant, leasehold improvements)
Property used predominantly (>50%) for business
Property purchased (not inherited or gifted)

sections.title: Section 179 Limitations
sections.content:
**Cannot exceed:**
1. Cost of qualifying property
2. Taxable income from all active trades/businesses

**Income Limitation:**
Cannot create or increase a net operating loss

**Exception:** Unused Section 179 carries forward indefinitely

**SUVs:** Limited to $30,500 (2024) for vehicles over 6,000 lbs GVW

sections.title: Bonus Depreciation
sections.content:
**What is Bonus Depreciation?**
Automatic first-year depreciation for qualifying property. Unlike Section 179, it's not an election (though can elect out).

**2024 Rate:** 60% (phasing down)
• 2023: 80%
• 2024: 60%
• 2025: 40%
• 2026: 20%
• 2027: 0%

**After bonus:** Remaining basis depreciated under MACRS

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Order of Application
sections.content:
**When applying depreciation methods:**

1. **Section 179** first (if elected)
2. **Bonus Depreciation** on remaining basis
3. **MACRS** on remaining basis after 1 and 2

**Example:**
• $100,000 equipment placed in service
• Elect $40,000 Section 179
• Bonus on remaining $60,000 × 60% = $36,000
• MACRS on remaining $24,000

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Section 179 limit: $1,220,000 (2024), phase-out begins at $3,050,000
Section 179 limited to business income - cannot create NOL
Bonus depreciation: 60% for 2024 (phasing down to 0% by 2027)
Bonus can create or increase NOL
SUV Section 179 limit: $30,500
Apply Section 179 first, then bonus, then MACRS
Both can apply to same asset in correct order

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2024, a taxpayer purchases and places in service $3,200,000 of qualifying Section 179 property. The taxpayer's taxable income from their active trade or business is $1,300,000. What is the maximum Section 179 deduction they can take?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The maximum Section 179 deduction is limited to the lesser of the maximum deduction ($1,220,000), the cost of qualifying property, and the taxable income from the trade or business. However, the $1,220,000 maximum is reduced by the amount the qualifying property exceeds the phase-out threshold of $3,050,000. In this case, $3,200,000 - $3,050,000 = $150,000. $1,220,000 - $150,000 = $1,070,000. The taxable income is $1,300,000, so the limit doesn't apply.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both the overall limit and the phase-out rules.

reveal.question: What is the maximum Section 179 deduction allowed for a qualifying SUV (over 6,000 lbs) placed in service in 2024?
reveal.answer: The maximum Section 179 deduction for a qualifying SUV (over 6,000 lbs) is $30,500 for 2024.

rows.label: Cost of Qualifying Property
values: The total cost of Section 179 property placed in service during the year.
The Section 179 deduction cannot exceed this amount.

rows.label: Taxable Income Limitation
values: Taxable income derived from the active conduct of any trade or business.
The Section 179 deduction cannot exceed this amount and cannot create or increase a net operating loss (NOL).

rows.label: Maximum Deduction Limit
values: The legislated maximum deduction amount.
The 2024 maximum is $1,220,000, but this amount may be reduced due to the phase-out rules.

title: Inventory Accounting
description: Master inventory methods and cost of goods sold calculations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory accounting directly affects taxable income! The method chosen can significantly impact taxes in periods of changing prices. Understanding these methods is essential for business clients.

sections.title: When Inventory Required
sections.content:
**Inventory required when:**
• Selling merchandise
• Manufacturing goods

**Small Business Exception:**
• Average annual gross receipts ≤ $29 million (2024)
• Can treat inventory as non-incidental materials/supplies
• Deduct when used or consumed

**If inventory required:** Must use accrual method for purchases and sales

sections.title: Inventory Methods
rows: Lower COGS, higher income, higher tax
Higher COGS, lower income, lower tax

sections.title: LIFO Special Rules
sections.content:
**LIFO Election:**
• Once elected, generally cannot change without IRS approval
• Must use for financial statements if used for tax (conformity rule)
• May produce lower tax in rising price environment

**LIFO Reserve:**
Difference between FIFO and LIFO inventory values. Can be significant!

**LIFO Recapture:**
If converting from C corp to S corp, must recapture LIFO reserve into income (over 4 years).

sections.title: Lower of Cost or Market (LCM)
sections.content:
**Can value inventory at lower of cost or market:**

**Market = Replacement Cost**
But limited to:
• Ceiling: Net realizable value (selling price minus disposal costs)
• Floor: NRV minus normal profit margin

**Cannot use with LIFO!**

**Used for:** Damaged, obsolete, or slow-moving inventory

sections.title: COGS Calculation
sections.content:
**Cost of Goods Sold:**

Beginning Inventory
+ Purchases
+ Direct Labor (manufacturing)
+ Other Costs (freight-in, allocation of overhead)
= Cost of Goods Available for Sale
- Ending Inventory
= **Cost of Goods Sold**

**Note:** COGS is an offset to gross receipts, not a deduction.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Inventory required for businesses selling merchandise
Small business exception: ≤ $29 million average gross receipts
FIFO = lower COGS in rising prices (higher tax)
LIFO = higher COGS in rising prices (lower tax)
LIFO conformity rule: must use for financial statements
LCM can write down damaged/obsolete inventory
LIFO recapture required on C-to-S conversion

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. uses LIFO for tax purposes. Can they use FIFO for reporting inventory on their financial statements to shareholders without IRS approval?
options: Yes, they can freely choose different methods.
No, the LIFO conformity rule requires them to also use LIFO for financial reporting.
Yes, but only if the difference between LIFO and FIFO is immaterial.
No, they must obtain IRS approval to use any other method for financial reporting.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The LIFO conformity rule mandates that if a company uses LIFO for tax purposes, it must also use LIFO for financial reporting to shareholders. This ensures consistency between tax and financial reporting.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the implications of the LIFO conformity rule.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company using FIFO has inventory with an original cost of $50 per unit. The current replacement cost is $40 per unit. The selling price is $60 per unit, with disposal costs of $5 per unit and a normal profit margin of $10 per unit. What is the market value under the LCM method?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate the Net Realizable Value (NRV): $60 (selling price) - $5 (disposal costs) = $55. Then, calculate the NRV less profit margin (Floor): $55 - $10 = $45. The Ceiling is $55, and the Floor is $45. The replacement cost ($40) is below the floor of $45, so the market value is the floor of $45. Therefore, the lower of cost ($50) or market ($45) is $45.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the Ceiling and Floor before comparing to replacement cost.

reveal.question: What is the maximum average annual gross receipts a business can have to be eligible for the small business exception that allows them to treat inventory as non-incidental materials and supplies?
reveal.answer: The average annual gross receipts must be $29 million or less (for 2024). If this threshold is met, the business can deduct the cost of inventory when it's used or consumed rather than following strict inventory accounting rules.

rows.label: Cost Flow Assumption
values: First goods purchased are the first goods sold.
Last goods purchased are the first goods sold.

rows.label: Tax Implications (Rising Prices)
values: Generally results in higher taxable income.
Generally results in lower taxable income.

rows.label: Balance Sheet Inventory Valuation (Rising Prices)
values: Inventory reflects more current costs.
Inventory may be significantly undervalued relative to current costs.

rows.label: Lower of Cost or Market
values: Allowed
Not Allowed

title: Business Interest Expense Limitation
description: Understand Section 163(j) business interest limitation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: TCJA limited business interest deductions! Large businesses can only deduct interest up to a percentage of income. Understanding these rules is critical for business clients with significant debt.

sections.title: Section 163(j) Overview
sections.content:
**Limitation Rule:**
Business interest expense deduction limited to:

**30% of Adjusted Taxable Income (ATI)**
Plus: Business interest income
Plus: Floor plan financing interest

**Excess is not lost** - carries forward indefinitely

sections.title: Calculating ATI
sections.content:
**Adjusted Taxable Income (ATI):**

Taxable income
+ Business interest expense
+ Net operating loss deduction
+ Section 199A deduction (QBI)
+ Depreciation, amortization, depletion (for years before 2022)

**Note:** Starting 2022, depreciation, amortization, and depletion are NOT added back. This reduced ATI for many businesses!

sections.title: Small Business Exception
sections.content:
**Exempt from limitation:**

Businesses with average annual gross receipts ≤ $29 million (2024) for prior 3 years.

**What this means:**
• Can deduct all business interest
• No ATI calculation required
• Same threshold as other small business simplifications

**Test applied at entity level** (aggregation rules may apply)

sections.title: Excluded Businesses
content: Real property trades or businesses (by election)
Farming businesses (by election)
Certain utilities
Small businesses (< $29 million gross receipts)
Note: Electing out requires using ADS depreciation for real property!

sections.title: Carryforward Rules
sections.content:
**Disallowed Interest:**
• Carries forward indefinitely
• Used when ATI allows
• No expiration

**Pass-through Entities:**
• Limitation applied at entity level
• Excess carryforward stays with entity
• Only allowed interest passes to owners

**Corporations:**
• Carryforward travels with corporation

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Business interest limited to 30% of ATI
Small business exception: ≤ $29 million average gross receipts
Starting 2022, depreciation not added back to ATI
Real property and farming businesses can elect out
Electing out requires ADS depreciation
Disallowed interest carries forward indefinitely

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. has $1,000,000 in business interest expense, $200,000 in business interest income, and $500,000 in Adjusted Taxable Income (ATI) for 2024. What is the maximum amount of business interest expense XYZ Corp. can deduct in 2024?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The business interest expense deduction is limited to 30% of ATI plus business interest income. In this case, 30% of $500,000 is $150,000. Adding the $200,000 of business interest income results in a deductible amount of $350,000. Since floor plan financing interest is not mentioned, we assume it is $0. The total deductible amount is therefore $150,000 + $200,000 = $350,000. Because the company has $1,000,000 of business interest expense, but only $350,000 is deductible, the maximum amount deductible is $350,000. The calculation should be (0.30 * $500,000) + $200,000. The maximum deduction is the lesser of the calculated amount or the interest expense which is not an issue in this case.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include business interest income in your calculation.

reveal.question: List five items added back to taxable income to arrive at Adjusted Taxable Income (ATI) before 2022.
reveal.answer:
1. Business interest expense
2. Net operating loss (NOL) deduction
3. Section 199A deduction (QBI)
4. Depreciation
5. Amortization
6. Depletion

sections.title: Small Business Exception vs. Section 163(j) Limitation

rows.label: Applicability
values: Applies if average annual gross receipts ≤ $29 million (2024) for the prior 3 years.
Applies if average annual gross receipts > $29 million (2024) for the prior 3 years.

rows.label: Interest Deduction
values: Can deduct all business interest expense.
Business interest expense deduction limited to 30% of ATI + business interest income + floor plan financing interest.

rows.label: ATI Calculation Required
values: No ATI calculation required.
ATI calculation is required to determine the deductible amount.

rows.label: Carryforward
values: Not Applicable
Excess interest expense can be carried forward indefinitely.

title: Section 1231, 1245, and 1250 Property
description: Master the depreciation recapture and Section 1231 netting rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When businesses sell assets, the character of gain matters! Section 1231 provides capital gain treatment for gains but ordinary loss for losses - the best of both worlds. But watch for recapture!

sections.title: Section 1231 Property
sections.content:
**Section 1231 Property Includes:**

• Depreciable business property held > 1 year
• Real property used in trade or business held > 1 year
• Timber, coal, and domestic iron ore
• Livestock held for draft, breeding, dairy, or sporting
• Unharvested crops

**NOT Section 1231:**
• Inventory
• Property held for sale to customers
• Copyrights, artistic compositions (by creator)

sections.title: Section 1231 Netting Rules
sections.content:
**The Best of Both Worlds:**

Step 1: Net all 1231 gains and losses

**If Net Gain:**
Treated as long-term capital gain (up to 20% rate)

**If Net Loss:**
Treated as ordinary loss (fully deductible against ordinary income)

**5-Year Lookback:**
Net 1231 gain is ordinary income to extent of unrecaptured net 1231 losses from prior 5 years.

sections.title: Section 1245 Recapture
sections.content:
**Applies to Personal Property:**

• Machinery, equipment, vehicles
• Office furniture
• Other tangible personal property

**Recapture Rule:**
Gain is ordinary income to extent of ALL depreciation taken.

**Example:**
• Machinery: $100,000 cost, $60,000 depreciation
• Adjusted basis: $40,000
• Sold for $85,000
• Gain: $45,000
• Section 1245 recapture: $45,000 (all ordinary)
• If sold for $110,000: $60,000 ordinary, $10,000 Section 1231 gain

sections.title: Section 1250 Recapture
sections.content:
**Applies to Real Property:**

• Buildings
• Structural components

**Historical Rule:**
Recapture excess of accelerated over straight-line depreciation as ordinary income.

**Current Reality:**
Since 1987, real property must use straight-line, so usually NO Section 1250 recapture.

**Unrecaptured Section 1250 Gain:**
The straight-line depreciation on real property is taxed at maximum 25% rate (not 15%/20% capital gain rate).

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Recapture Order
sections.content:
**For business asset sales:**

1. **Section 1245** - All depreciation on personal property = ordinary
2. **Section 1250** - Excess depreciation on real property = ordinary (rare now)
3. **Unrecaptured 1250** - Straight-line depreciation on real = 25% cap gain
4. **Section 1231** - Remaining gain = LTCG; Net loss = ordinary

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Section 1231: Net gain = capital gain; Net loss = ordinary loss
5-year lookback converts current gain to ordinary
Section 1245: Personal property, all depreciation recaptured as ordinary
Section 1250: Real property, excess depreciation recaptured (rare now)
Unrecaptured 1250 gain: 25% maximum rate
Order: 1245 recapture → 1250 recapture → Unrecaptured 1250 → 1231

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following assets, if held for more than one year and used in a trade or business, would be considered Section 1245 property?
options: A commercial building
Land used as a parking lot for the business
Office furniture
Timber
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Section 1245 property primarily includes depreciable personal property, such as office furniture. Real property, like a commercial building or land, falls under Section 1250 or Section 1231. Timber is specifically included as Section 1231 property, not Section 1245.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider whether the asset is real or personal property and whether it is subject to depreciation.

reveal.question: Explain the purpose and application of the 5-year lookback rule in the context of Section 1231 gains.
reveal.answer: The 5-year lookback rule prevents taxpayers from converting ordinary income into capital gains through the use of Section 1231. Any net Section 1231 gain is treated as ordinary income to the extent of any Section 1231 losses deducted in the five preceding tax years. This recharacterization applies only to the *extent* of those previously deducted losses.

rows.label: Property Type
values: Depreciable personal property (e.g., machinery, equipment)
Depreciable real property (e.g., buildings)

rows.label: Recapture Amount
values: Gain is ordinary income to the extent of *all* depreciation taken.

Gain is ordinary income to the extent of accelerated depreciation *exceeding* straight-line depreciation (for certain property held before 1987). For property placed in service after 1986, all depreciation is potentially subject to recapture, but typically only to the extent of depreciation claimed.

rows.label: Typical Taxpayer Impact
values: More likely to have full recapture of depreciation
Less likely to have full recapture of depreciation, especially for property placed in service after 1986 and depreciated using the straight-line method.

title: Net Operating Losses
description: Understand NOL calculation and carryforward rules

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: NOLs let businesses offset future income with current losses! Understanding the current rules (post-TCJA, as modified) is essential for tax planning when businesses have loss years.

sections.title: What is an NOL?
sections.content:
**Net Operating Loss:**
When business deductions exceed business income.

**For Individuals:**
NOL = Negative taxable income after certain modifications:
• Add back personal exemptions
• Add back non-business deductions exceeding non-business income
• Add back NOL deduction from other years
• Add back capital losses exceeding capital gains

**Corporations:**
Simpler calculation - negative taxable income without DRD limitation modifications.

sections.title: Current NOL Rules (Post-TCJA)
sections.content:
**For NOLs arising after 2020:**

• **No carryback** (general rule)
• **Unlimited carryforward**
• **80% limitation**: Can only offset 80% of taxable income in carryforward year

**Exception - Farming Losses:**
• Can carry back 2 years
• 80% limitation still applies in carryforward years

**Exception - Certain Insurance Companies:**
Different rules apply

sections.title: Using NOL Carryforwards
sections.content:
**Ordering:**
• Oldest NOL used first (FIFO)
• Apply to 80% of modified taxable income
• Remainder carries forward

**Example:**
• 2024 taxable income (before NOL): $100,000
• NOL carryforward available: $150,000
• Maximum NOL deduction: $80,000 (80% × $100,000)
• Taxable income after NOL: $20,000
• Remaining NOL carryforward: $70,000

sections.title: NOL for Pass-Through Entities
sections.content:
**Partnerships and S Corporations:**
No entity-level NOL. Losses pass through to owners and may create NOL on their individual returns.

**Limitations apply at owner level:**
• Basis limitations
• At-risk limitations
• Passive activity loss rules
• Excess business loss limitation

**Only after passing all these can loss become part of individual's NOL.**

sections.title: Excess Business Loss Limitation
sections.content:
**Section 461(l):**

For non-corporate taxpayers, business losses limited to:
• Business income PLUS
• $305,000 (single) / $610,000 (MFJ) for 2024

**Excess becomes:**
NOL carryforward (subject to 80% rule)

**Applies AFTER:**
Basis, at-risk, and passive activity rules

**In effect through 2028** (unless extended)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NOLs carry forward indefinitely (no carryback for most)
80% limitation: Can only offset 80% of income
Farming losses can carry back 2 years
Oldest NOLs used first (FIFO)
Pass-through losses flow to owners and may create individual NOL
Excess business loss limitation: $305K/$610K (2024)

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2024, Sarah's business has taxable income of $75,000 before considering any NOL carryforwards. She has an NOL carryforward from 2021 of $90,000. What is the maximum NOL deduction Sarah can take in 2024?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The maximum NOL deduction is limited to 80% of taxable income. In this case, 80% of $75,000 is $60,000. Even though the NOL carryforward is $90,000, only $60,000 can be deducted.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the 80% limitation on NOL carryforwards.

reveal.question: What is the general rule for carrying back NOLs arising after 2020?
reveal.answer: Generally, NOLs arising after 2020 cannot be carried back. The exception is for farming losses, which can be carried back 2 years.

rows.label: Carryback
values: No Carryback (after 2020)
Carryback 2 Years

rows.label: Carryforward
values: Unlimited
Unlimited

rows.label: Limitation
values: 80% of Taxable Income
80% of Taxable Income

knowledgeCheck.question: John has two NOL carryforwards: $20,000 from 2022 and $30,000 from 2023. In 2024, he is able to deduct $40,000 of NOLs. How much of the 2022 NOL carryforward remains?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The oldest NOL is used first (FIFO). Therefore, the $20,000 from 2022 is used before the $30,000 from 2023. Since John deducted $40,000 total, the entire $20,000 from 2022 is used, leaving a balance of $0.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember FIFO - First In, First Out.

title: Loss Limitation Rules
description: Master at-risk rules and passive activity loss limitations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Not all business losses are immediately deductible! Understanding the hierarchy of loss limitations ensures correct reporting and helps clients plan to use their losses effectively.

sections.title: Loss Limitation Hierarchy
sections.content:
**Order of Application:**

1. **Basis Limitation** - Can't deduct more than basis
2. **At-Risk Limitation** - Only amounts at risk
3. **Passive Activity Loss Rules** - Only against passive income
4. **Excess Business Loss** - $305K/$610K cap

**Each limitation applied in order!**
Loss failing any level is suspended until that barrier clears.

sections.title: At-Risk Rules (Section 465)
sections.content:
**Amount At Risk Includes:**
• Cash contributed
• Adjusted basis of property contributed
• Amounts borrowed for which personally liable
• Amounts borrowed secured by property in activity

**NOT At Risk:**
• Nonrecourse debt (with real estate exception)
• Amounts protected against loss
• Amounts borrowed from related parties

**Losses limited to amount at risk.** Excess suspended until more at risk.

sections.title: Passive Activity Loss Rules
sections.content:
**General Rule:**
Passive losses can only offset passive income.

**What is Passive Activity?**
• Any trade or business in which taxpayer doesn't materially participate
• Any rental activity (with exceptions)

**Material Participation:**
Must meet one of seven tests (most common: 500 hours during year)

**Suspended losses:**
• Carry forward
• Released when activity disposed of in fully taxable transaction

sections.title: Material Participation Tests
rows: More than 500 hours during the year
Substantially all participation in the activity
More than 100 hours and at least as much as anyone else
Significant participation in multiple activities totaling 500+ hours
Material participation in any 5 of prior 10 years
Personal service activity - any 3 prior years

sections.title: Real Estate Professionals Exception
sections.content:
**Rental activities are NOT automatically passive if:**

• More than 50% of personal services in real property trades/businesses
• More than 750 hours in real property trades/businesses
• Materially participates in rental activities

**Result:** Rental losses can offset non-passive income!

**Warning:** Must meet ALL requirements. Heavily audited by IRS.

sections.title: $25,000 Rental Loss Allowance
sections.content:
**Active Participation Exception:**

Up to $25,000 of rental losses can offset non-passive income if:
• Actively participate (own 10%+, participate in management)
• Not a limited partner

**Phase-out:**
• Begins at $100,000 MAGI
• Fully phased out at $150,000
• Reduced $1 for every $2 over $100,000

**Example:**
$125,000 MAGI = $12,500 allowance

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Loss limitations apply in order: Basis → At-Risk → PAL → EBL
At-risk: Must be personally liable or have basis in property
Passive = no material participation (500 hours is key test)
Passive losses only offset passive income
$25,000 active rental exception (phases out $100K-$150K MAGI)
Real estate professionals can treat rentals as non-passive
Suspended losses released on fully taxable disposition

knowledgeCheck.question: John invests $50,000 cash in a business and borrows $100,000 on a recourse note (personally liable). He also borrows $75,000 on a nonrecourse note secured by property he uses in the business. How much is John considered at-risk for in this business?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: John is at-risk for the cash contribution ($50,000) and the recourse debt ($100,000). Nonrecourse debt generally isn't included in the at-risk amount. Therefore, his at-risk amount is $150,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to distinguish between recourse and nonrecourse debt.

reveal.question: List the order in which loss limitations are applied.
reveal.answer:
1. Basis Limitation
2. At-Risk Limitation
3. Passive Activity Loss Rules
4. Excess Business Loss Limitation

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah has a passive loss of $20,000 from a rental property and passive income of $5,000 from a limited partnership interest. She also has $60,000 of wage income. What amount of the passive loss can Sarah deduct this year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah can only deduct passive losses against passive income. She has $5,000 of passive income, so she can deduct $5,000 of the passive loss. The remaining $15,000 is carried forward.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Passive losses can only offset passive income.

title: Qualified Business Income Deduction
description: Master Section 199A pass-through deduction

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Section 199A provides up to a 20% deduction for pass-through business income! This is a major tax benefit for sole proprietors, partners, and S corporation shareholders. Know the rules!

sections.title: Basic Deduction
sections.content:
**Section 199A Deduction:**

**20% of Qualified Business Income (QBI)**

From:
• Sole proprietorships
• Partnerships
• S corporations
• REITs (qualified dividends)
• PTPs (publicly traded partnerships)

**Below-the-line deduction** (reduces taxable income, not AGI)

sections.title: QBI Definition
sections.content:
**Qualified Business Income:**
Net amount of qualified items from a qualified trade or business.

**Includes:**
• Ordinary income/loss
• Section 1231 gain/loss

**Does NOT Include:**
• Capital gains and losses
• Dividends and interest (unless banking)
• Guaranteed payments to partners
• Reasonable compensation (S corp)
• Investment income
• Wage income

sections.title: Below Threshold
sections.content:
**If taxable income ≤ threshold:**

• 20% of QBI from each business
• No W-2 wages or property limitations
• SSTB status doesn't matter
• No additional calculations required

**Plus:** 20% of qualified REIT dividends and PTP income

**Limited to:** 20% of (taxable income minus net capital gain)

sections.title: Above Threshold - Limitations
sections.content:
**Two Limitations Apply:**

**1. W-2 Wage/Property Limit:**
Deduction limited to greater of:
• 50% of W-2 wages paid by business, OR
• 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA of qualified property

**2. SSTB Limitation:**
Specified Service Trades or Businesses:
• Deduction phases out between threshold and threshold + $50K (single) / $100K (MFJ)
• Completely disallowed above phase-out

**SSTB includes:** Health, law, accounting, consulting, financial services, performing arts, athletics, etc.

sections.title: SSTB List
content: Health (doctors, dentists, nurses, physical therapists)
Law (attorneys, paralegals)
Accounting (CPAs, bookkeepers)
Actuarial science
Performing arts (musicians, actors)
Consulting (unless in manufacturing, etc.)
Athletics (players, coaches)
Financial services (investment advice, etc.)
Brokerage services
Any business where principal asset is reputation/skill of owners

sections.title: ⚠️ SSTB Exclusions
sections.content:
**NOT an SSTB:**
• Architecture
• Engineering
• Real estate agents/brokers
• Insurance agents
• Banking

**Key Distinction:**
Consulting where the product is something other than advice (e.g., manufacturing consultant who provides production plans) may not be SSTB.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 20% deduction for qualified business income from pass-throughs
Below threshold: No W-2 wages or SSTB limitations
Above threshold: W-2 wages/property limitation applies
SSTB: Deduction phases out and is disallowed above threshold
Limited to 20% of (taxable income minus net capital gains)
Reasonable compensation and guaranteed payments excluded from QBI
Expires after 2025 unless extended (TCJA sunset)

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following items is NOT included in the calculation of Qualified Business Income (QBI) for the Section 199A deduction?
options: Ordinary income from a sole proprietorship.
Section 1231 gain from the sale of business property.
Dividends received from a publicly traded corporation.
Ordinary loss from a partnership.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Dividends are generally excluded from QBI, unless they are related to banking activities. Ordinary income/loss and Section 1231 gains/losses are included.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the nature of the income and whether it's directly related to the business's core operations.

reveal.question: What is the definition of Qualified Business Income (QBI) for Section 199A purposes?
reveal.answer: QBI is the net amount of qualified items of income, gain, deduction, and loss from a qualified trade or business. It generally includes ordinary income and loss, as well as Section 1231 gains and losses, but excludes items like capital gains/losses, dividends (unless from banking), and guaranteed payments to partners.

rows.label: W-2 Wages/Property Limitation
values: No limitation applies
Limitation may apply

rows.label: SSTB Relevance
values: SSTB status does not impact the calculation.
SSTB status may impact the calculation, potentially reducing or eliminating the deduction.

rows.label: Complexity of Calculation
values: Simpler calculation; deduction is 20% of QBI (plus 20% of REIT dividends and PTP income), limited to 20% of taxable income less net capital gain.
More complex calculation; requires consideration of W-2 wages, unadjusted basis of qualified property, and potential phase-in/phase-out rules.

title: Partnership Taxation Fundamentals
description: Master the basics of partnership formation, operations, and reporting

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships are the most flexible business entities but have complex tax rules! Understanding formation, operations, basis, and distributions is tested extensively on the SEE.

sections.title: Partnership Fundamentals
sections.content:
**What is a Partnership for Tax Purposes?**
Two or more persons carrying on a trade or business as co-owners.

**Key Principle:** Aggregate Theory (mostly)
• Partnership is a conduit for income
• Partners pay tax, not the partnership
• Items flow through to partners

**Filing:**
• Form 1065 (information return)
• Schedule K-1 to each partner
• Due March 15 (calendar year)

sections.title: Formation: Contributing Property
sections.content:
**General Rule (Section 721):**
No gain or loss recognized when contributing property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest.

**Partner's Initial Basis:**
Basis in property contributed + cash contributed

**Partnership's Basis in Contributed Property:**
Carryover basis from contributing partner

**Exception - Gain Recognized:**
• Services in exchange for capital interest (ordinary income)
• Investment partnership with appreciated securities

sections.title: Partner Basis
sections.content:
**Initial Basis:**
Cash + adjusted basis of property contributed

**Increases:**
• Additional contributions
• Distributive share of income (including tax-exempt)
• Increase in share of liabilities

**Decreases:**
• Distributions received
• Distributive share of losses
• Decrease in share of liabilities
• Nondeductible non-capital expenditures

**Cannot Go Below Zero:** Losses suspended if no basis

sections.title: Partnership Liabilities and Basis
sections.content:
**Recourse Liabilities:**
• Partner bears economic risk of loss
• Allocated to partner who would pay if partnership couldn't
• Usually general partners

**Nonrecourse Liabilities:**
• No partner bears economic risk
• Allocated based on profit-sharing ratios (generally)
• Includes minimum gain allocations

**Effect:** Increase in share of liabilities = deemed cash contribution (increases basis)

sections.title: Distributive Share
sections.content:
**Partnership Agreement Controls** (if substantial economic effect)

**What Flows Through to Partners:**
• Ordinary income/loss from operations
• Capital gains and losses (separately stated)
• Section 1231 gains/losses
• Charitable contributions
• Section 179 deduction
• Investment interest expense
• Tax-exempt income
• Many other items

**Timing:** Partners report in year partnership's tax year ends (not when received)

sections.title: ⚠️ Guaranteed Payments
sections.content:
**Guaranteed Payments:**
Payments to a partner for services or capital use without regard to partnership income.

**Treatment:**
• Deductible by partnership (like wages)
• Ordinary income to partner
• Partner includes in year of partnership's year-end
• Subject to self-employment tax

**Note:** NOT included in basis calculations until flowed through on K-1!

sections.title: Partnership Distributions
sections.content:
**Current Distributions:**
• Not taxable if within basis
• Reduce partner's basis
• Cash exceeding basis = gain
• Property received = carryover basis

**Liquidating Distributions:**
• May trigger gain or loss
• Gain only if cash > basis
• Loss only if distribution is only cash, inventory, unrealized receivables

**Special Rule:** Distribution of property generally keeps carryover basis (limited to partner's remaining basis)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnerships are pass-through - partners pay tax on their share
Formation: generally no gain/loss on property contribution
Partner basis: starts with contribution, adjusts for income/loss/distributions
Share of liabilities increases/decreases basis
Guaranteed payments: ordinary income to partner, deductible by partnership
Current distributions: not taxable unless cash exceeds basis
Losses limited to partner's basis (cannot go below zero)

sections.title: Partnership Formation and Basis: Check Your Understanding

knowledgeCheck.question: Alice contributes land with an adjusted basis of $50,000 and a fair market value of $80,000 to a partnership in exchange for a 25% interest. Bob contributes $30,000 cash for a 25% interest. Charlie and Diane contribute services. What is Alice's initial basis in her partnership interest?
options: The basis is determined at the end of the partnership's tax year
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Generally, when contributing property to a partnership in exchange for a partnership interest, no gain or loss is recognized. The partner's initial basis is the adjusted basis of the property contributed. Therefore, Alice's initial basis is $50,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the general rule of Section 721 regarding contributions to a partnership.

sections.title: Recall: Partner Basis Increases and Decreases

reveal.question: List three factors that INCREASE a partner's basis in their partnership interest.
reveal.answer:
1. Additional contributions
2. Distributive share of partnership income (including tax-exempt income)
3. Increase in the partner's share of partnership liabilities.

sections.title: Partnership Liabilities and Basis: Check Your Understanding

knowledgeCheck.question: A partner's share of partnership liabilities increases by $10,000 during the year. Which of the following statements is correct regarding the impact on the partner's basis?
options: The partner's basis will decrease by $10,000.
The partner's basis will increase by $10,000.
The change in liabilities has no effect on the partner's basis.
The partner's basis will increase only if the partnership is profitable.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An increase in a partner's share of partnership liabilities is treated as a contribution of cash by the partner to the partnership. This increases the partner's basis in their partnership interest.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think of an increase in liabilities as the partner taking on more debt, effectively contributing more capital to the partnership.

rows.label: Filing Entity
values: Partnership
Partner (attached to their individual return)

rows.label: Purpose
values: Reports partnership's income, deductions, gains, and losses.
Reports the partner's share of the partnership's income, deductions, credits, etc.

rows.label: Recipient
values: IRS
Partner and IRS

title: S Corporation Fundamentals
description: Understand S corporation election, requirements, and taxation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corporations offer the best of both worlds: corporate liability protection with pass-through taxation. But strict rules govern eligibility and operations. Get this wrong and the election terminates!

sections.title: Making the S Election
sections.content:
**Form 2553: Election by a Small Business Corporation**

**Timing:**
• By March 15 for existing corporation (calendar year)
• Any time in preceding year
• Within 2 months 15 days of formation (new corp)

**All shareholders must consent**

**Late Election Relief:**
• Available if reasonable cause
• Must file within 3 years 75 days of intended effective date

sections.title: Shareholder Basis
sections.content:
**Initial Basis:**
Cost of stock + cash/property contributed

**Increases:**
• Additional capital contributions
• Distributive share of income (including tax-exempt)

**Decreases:**
• Distributions
• Distributive share of losses
• Nondeductible/non-capital expenses

**IMPORTANT Difference from Partnerships:**
• Shareholder loans increase debt basis (separate from stock basis)
• Can deduct losses against stock AND debt basis
• But S corp liabilities don't increase stock basis!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: S Corp vs Partnership Basis
sections.content:
**The Critical Difference:**

**Partnership:** Liabilities (recourse and nonrecourse) increase partner's basis

**S Corporation:** Corporate liabilities do NOT increase shareholder basis

**Only shareholder loans to the corporation increase debt basis!**

This is a commonly tested distinction!

sections.title: Reasonable Compensation Requirement
sections.content:
**Why It Matters:**
Shareholders who provide services MUST receive reasonable compensation.

**The Game:** Pay low salary = less employment tax

**IRS Attack:** If compensation is too low:
• Reclassify distributions as wages
• Assess back employment taxes, penalties, interest

**Factors for "Reasonable":**
• Comparable wages in similar businesses
• Training and experience
• Duties and responsibilities
• Time devoted

sections.title: Built-In Gains Tax
sections.content:
**Applies to C corps converting to S corps:**

If corporation held appreciated assets when it elected S status:
• Tax at 21% on net recognized built-in gains
• Recognition period: 5 years after S election
• Only applies to pre-election appreciation

**Limitation:** Cannot exceed taxable income if S corp the entire year

**Planning:** Wait 5 years before selling appreciated assets, or elect S status when values are low.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: S corp requires ≤100 shareholders, one class of stock, domestic
No nonresident aliens or ineligible entity shareholders
File Form 2553 by March 15 for calendar year corp
Corporate liabilities do NOT increase shareholder basis
Shareholder-employees must receive reasonable compensation
Built-in gains tax applies 5 years after C-to-S conversion
Losses limited to stock basis + shareholder loan basis

sections.title: S Election Timing: Test Your Knowledge

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation, a calendar-year entity, was formed on November 1, 2023. To be treated as an S corporation beginning January 1, 2024, the corporation must file Form 2553 no later than:
options: January 15, 2024.
January 16, 2024.
February 15, 2024.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The S election must be filed within 2 months and 15 days of formation. For ABC Corporation, formed on November 1, 2023, this deadline is January 16, 2024 (November 1 + 2 months and 15 days).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the 2-month, 15-day rule for new corporations.

reveal.question: List three factors that INCREASE a shareholder's basis in an S corporation.
reveal.answer:
1. Additional capital contributions.
2. Distributive share of income (including tax-exempt income).
3. Loans made by the shareholder to the S corporation.

knowledgeCheck.question: John is a shareholder in an S corporation. During the year, the corporation reported the following: Distributive share of income: $50,000, Cash distribution to John: $30,000, Nondeductible expenses: $5,000. John's initial basis in the S corporation was $20,000. What is John's basis at year-end?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: John's basis is calculated as follows: Initial basis ($20,000) + Distributive share of income ($50,000) - Distributions ($30,000) - Nondeductible expenses ($5,000) = $35,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the order of basis adjustments: increase for income, then decrease for distributions and nondeductible expenses.

title: Partnership Distributions
description: Master the rules for partnership distributions to partners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnership distributions are complex! Understanding when gain is recognized and how basis is affected is crucial for advising partners on tax consequences of receiving cash or property.

sections.title: Money Distributions
sections.content:
**Cash Distributions:**

• Reduce partner's basis dollar for dollar
• If cash exceeds basis = capital gain
• No loss on cash distribution alone

**Marketable Securities:**
Generally treated as money (with some exceptions)

**Example:**
• Partner basis: $10,000
• Cash distributed: $15,000
• Result: $5,000 capital gain
• New basis: $0

sections.title: Property Distributions
sections.content:
**General Rule - No Gain or Loss:**

**Partner's Basis in Property:**
• Current distribution: Lesser of partnership's basis OR partner's remaining outside basis
• Liquidating distribution: Equal to remaining outside basis (substituted)

**Partnership's Basis:**
Goes to partner (subject to limitations)

**No gain to partner** unless cash + marketable securities > basis

sections.title: Hot Assets - Section 751
sections.content:
**Section 751(b) applies when:**
Distribution causes shift in hot assets between partners.

**Hot Assets:**
• Unrealized receivables (A/R, recapture)
• Inventory items

**Effect:**
Portion of distribution treated as a taxable exchange between partner and partnership.

**Creates ordinary income/loss**, not capital.

sections.title: Liquidating Distributions
sections.content:
**Partner's Interest Terminates:**

**Loss Recognition:**
Only if:
• Only cash, inventory, and unrealized receivables distributed
• Partner's basis exceeds FMV of distribution

**Basis in Property:**
Substituted basis = partner's outside basis reduced by cash

**Allocation if Multiple Properties:**
• First to unrealized receivables and inventory (at partnership basis)
• Remaining basis to other assets (may require allocation adjustments)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Cash > basis = capital gain
Property distributions: generally no gain to partner
Current distribution: property takes lesser of carryover or remaining basis
Liquidating distribution: substituted basis
Section 751 hot assets can create ordinary income
Loss only on liquidating distribution of cash/inventory/receivables

knowledgeCheck.question: Partner Able has a basis of $8,000 in his partnership interest. He receives a cash distribution of $12,000. What is the tax consequence to Able?
options: No gain or loss is recognized.
Able recognizes a capital gain of $4,000.
Able recognizes ordinary income of $4,000.
Able recognizes a capital loss of $4,000.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: When a cash distribution exceeds a partner's basis, the partner recognizes a capital gain to the extent of the excess. In this case, the distribution exceeds the basis by $4,000 ($12,000 - $8,000), resulting in a $4,000 capital gain.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the rule about cash distributions exceeding basis!

reveal.question: Define what constitutes 'hot assets' under Section 751 for partnership distributions.
reveal.answer: Hot assets include unrealized receivables (such as accounts receivable and depreciation recapture) and inventory items. Distributions that cause a shift in these assets between partners can trigger ordinary income.

sections.title: Current vs. Liquidating Distributions: Partner's Basis in Property

rows.label: Partner's Basis in Distributed Property
values: Lesser of partnership's basis in the property OR partner's remaining outside basis.
Equal to the partner's remaining outside basis (substituted basis).

rows.label: Impact on Partner's Outside Basis
values: Reduced by the basis the partner takes in the distributed property (but not below zero).
Reduced to zero.

title: Schedule K-1 Partnership Items
description: Understand the partner's Schedule K-1 and separately stated items

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The K-1 is the roadmap for partner reporting! Understanding what each item means and how it flows to the partner's return is essential for preparing both partnership and partner returns.

sections.title: Why Items Are Separately Stated
sections.content:
**Character Preservation:**
Items are separately stated if the tax treatment depends on the partner's individual circumstances.

**Examples:**
• Capital gains (may have different rates, or capital losses to offset)
• Charitable contributions (subject to partner's AGI limitations)
• Section 179 deduction (subject to partner's income limitation)
• Investment interest (subject to partner's investment income)

**Ordinary business income:** Items that don't require separate treatment are netted together.

sections.title: Key K-1 Items
rows: Schedule E, may affect SE tax
Ordinary income, subject to SE tax

sections.title: Self-Employment Income
sections.content:
**General Partners:**
• Ordinary income from Box 1 subject to SE tax
• Guaranteed payments subject to SE tax
• Unless rental or passive activity

**Limited Partners:**
• Generally NOT subject to SE tax (no material participation)
• Guaranteed payments for services ARE subject to SE tax

**Box 14 tells the story:**
Shows partner's share of self-employment earnings for Schedule SE.

sections.title: Basis Adjustments from K-1
sections.content:
**Increase Basis:**
• Ordinary income (Box 1)
• Capital gains (Box 8-9)
• Tax-exempt income (Box 18)
• Increase in partner's share of liabilities

**Decrease Basis:**
• Distributions (Box 19)
• Losses/deductions (various boxes)
• Decrease in share of liabilities
• Nondeductible expenses (Box 18)

**Partner must track outside basis!** Not reported on K-1.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Separately stated items preserve character at partner level
Ordinary business income/loss flows to Schedule E
Guaranteed payments are always ordinary income
General partners: ordinary income usually subject to SE tax
Limited partners: generally exempt from SE tax (except guaranteed payments)
Partner must track outside basis (not shown on K-1)
Box 18-20: tax-exempt income, distributions, other items to track

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason why certain partnership items are separately stated on Schedule K-1?
options: To preserve the character of the item as it passes through to the partner.
To allow partners to apply their individual tax circumstances to the item.
To simplify the partnership's tax return preparation.
To ensure proper application of partner-level limitations (e.g., charitable contribution limits).
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Separately stated items are reported to preserve the character of the item and allow for partner-level application of tax laws and limitations. Simplifying the partnership's tax return is NOT a primary reason.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the ultimate goal of reporting partnership income and deductions.

sections.title: General vs. Limited Partners: Self-Employment Tax

rows.label: Ordinary Income (Box 1)
values: Subject to SE tax (unless rental or passive)
Generally NOT subject to SE tax

rows.label: Guaranteed Payments for Services
values: Subject to SE tax
Subject to SE tax

rows.label: Material Participation
values: Usually involved
Generally not involved

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following will DECREASE a partner's basis in their partnership interest?
options: The partner's share of the partnership's ordinary income (Box 1).
The partner's share of the partnership's capital gains (Box 8-9).
A distribution of cash from the partnership to the partner (Box 19).
An increase in the partner's share of partnership liabilities.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Distributions of cash from the partnership to the partner (Box 19) reduce the partner's basis. The other options increase the partner's basis.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what represents a return *of* capital vs. a contribution *to* capital.

title: Family Partnerships
description: Understand special rules for family partnerships

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The IRS scrutinizes family partnerships because they can be used to shift income to lower-bracket family members. Understanding the special rules ensures legitimate family partnerships are respected.

sections.title: General Rules
sections.content:
**When Family Member Recognized as Partner:**

• Capital is a material income-producing factor, AND
• Family member is actual owner of partnership interest

**OR**

• Family member performs services as partner
• Provides capital or skills materially important

sections.title: Capital as Material Factor
sections.content:
**Capital IS Material Factor:**
• Manufacturing
• Retail
• Requiring substantial inventory
• Requiring substantial equipment

**Capital is NOT Material Factor:**
• Service businesses (law, medicine, accounting)
• Where income comes primarily from services

**If capital isn't material:** Family member must actually perform services to be recognized as partner.

sections.title: Allocation Rules
sections.content:
**Donor Must Be Reasonably Compensated:**

Before allocating income to donee:
• Donor partner must receive reasonable compensation for services
• Remaining income allocated based on capital

**Example:**
• Partnership income: $200,000
• Donor's reasonable compensation for services: $80,000
• Remaining $120,000 allocated based on capital ownership

sections.title: Gift of Partnership Interest
sections.content:
**Donee's Basis:**
• Carryover basis from donor (gift basis rules)
• Includes share of partnership liabilities

**Gift Tax Implications:**
• FMV of interest may exceed basis
• Valuation discounts may apply

**Income Allocation:**
• Must follow reasonable compensation rules
• Cannot shift more income than donee's capital would generate

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Family partnerships face special scrutiny
Capital must be material income factor, or family member must perform services
Donor must receive reasonable compensation for services first
Remaining income allocated based on capital
Gift of partnership interest: donee gets carryover basis
Service businesses: harder to include non-working family members

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following conditions MUST be met for a family member to be recognized as a partner in a partnership where capital is a material income-producing factor?
options: The family member contributes only services to the partnership.
The family member is the actual owner of a partnership interest acquired by gift or purchase from another family member.
The family member is the actual owner of a partnership interest and performs substantial services for the partnership.
The family member is related to another partner in the partnership.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: For a family member to be recognized as a partner when capital is a material income-producing factor, they must be the actual owner of the partnership interest. Whether they acquired that interest by gift or purchase, ownership is key. The other conditions may be relevant in other partnership scenarios but are not necessarily required when capital is material.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the core requirement for partnership recognition: genuine ownership.

sections.title: Capital as a Material Income-Producing Factor: Recall

reveal.question: List three types of businesses where capital is generally considered a material income-producing factor.
reveal.answer:
1. Manufacturing
2. Retail
3. Businesses requiring substantial inventory or equipment

knowledgeCheck.question: A family partnership has an income of $300,000. The donor partner provides services to the partnership, and reasonable compensation for these services is determined to be $120,000. How is the remaining partnership income allocated?
options: The entire remaining income of $180,000 is allocated to the donor partner.
The remaining income of $180,000 is allocated based on the partners' capital interests.
The remaining income of $180,000 is allocated equally among all partners, regardless of capital interests.
The allocation method is at the discretion of the donor partner.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: After the donor partner receives reasonable compensation for their services, the remaining partnership income is allocated based on the partners' capital interests. This ensures a fair distribution based on the capital each partner has invested in the business.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Recall the priority of compensation before capital-based allocation.

title: Sale of Partnership Interest
description: Master the tax treatment of selling a partnership interest

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Selling a partnership interest is more complex than selling stock! Hot assets can convert capital gain to ordinary income, and the resulting basis issues affect both buyer and remaining partners.

sections.title: General Rule
sections.content:
**Sale of Partnership Interest:**
• Generally produces capital gain or loss
• Gain = Amount realized - Outside basis
• Long-term if held more than one year

**Amount Realized Includes:**
• Cash and property received
