• PLUS: Share of partnership liabilities transferred to buyer

**Outside Basis Includes:**
• Adjusted basis
• PLUS: Selling partner's share of liabilities (about to be reduced)

sections.title: Section 751 - Hot Assets
sections.content:
**Hot Assets Portion is ORDINARY:**

**Hot Assets:**
• Unrealized receivables
• Inventory items (substantially appreciated)

**Effect:**
Partner must recognize ordinary income to extent gain attributable to hot assets.

**Example:**
• Total gain: $100,000
• Portion attributable to hot assets: $30,000
• Ordinary income: $30,000
• Capital gain: $70,000

sections.title: Unrealized Receivables
content: Accounts receivable (cash method partnership)
Section 1245 recapture potential
Section 1250 recapture potential
Rights to payment for services or goods not yet recognized
Mining property recapture
Other ordinary income property

sections.title: Section 754 Election
sections.content:
**Optional Basis Adjustment:**

If partnership makes Section 754 election:
• Buyer gets special basis adjustment (Section 743)
• Adjusts buyer's share of inside basis to match outside basis

**Why it matters:**
Without 754 election, buyer's share of partnership basis may differ from what they paid.

**Once made:** Election applies to all future transfers (or until revoked).

**Mandatory 754:** Required if substantial built-in loss (>$250,000)

sections.title: Example: 754 Election Impact
sections.content:
**Without 754 Election:**
• Buyer pays $100,000 for 1/3 interest
• Partnership has $150,000 in assets (inside basis)
• Buyer's share of inside basis: $50,000
• Mismatch: $50,000

**With 754 Election:**
• Buyer gets $50,000 step-up (Section 743(b))
• Buyer's share of inside basis now equals what they paid
• No gain on later sale if values stay same

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sale of partnership interest: generally capital gain/loss
Amount realized includes buyer's assumption of liabilities
Section 751 hot assets create ordinary income
Hot assets: unrealized receivables, substantially appreciated inventory
Section 754 election adjusts buyer's inside basis
754 election is partnership-wide and continuing
Mandatory if partnership has substantial built-in loss

knowledgeCheck.question: Partner Able sells his partnership interest for $150,000 cash. He also transfers his share of partnership liabilities, which is $30,000, to the buyer. Able's outside basis in the partnership is $100,000. The portion of Able's gain attributable to hot assets is $20,000. What is the amount of Able's capital gain?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The amount realized is $150,000 (cash) + $30,000 (liabilities) = $180,000. The total gain is $180,000 (amount realized) - $100,000 (outside basis) = $80,000. The ordinary income is $20,000 (hot assets). Therefore, the capital gain is $80,000 - $20,000 = $60,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to include the liabilities transferred in the amount realized and subtract the ordinary income portion.

reveal.question: List the two categories of 'hot assets' under Section 751.
reveal.answer:
1. Unrealized Receivables.
2. Inventory items (substantially appreciated).

sections.title: Capital Gain vs. Ordinary Income in Partnership Interest Sale

rows.label: Source
values: Sale of partnership interest (excluding hot assets portion)
Portion of the sale attributable to hot assets (unrealized receivables and substantially appreciated inventory)

rows.label: Tax Rate
values: Preferential capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income level)
Ordinary income tax rates (up to 37% in 2023)

rows.label: Impact on Overall Tax Liability
values: Generally results in lower tax liability compared to ordinary income.
Can significantly increase tax liability due to higher tax rates.

title: S Corporation Distributions and AAA
description: Master distributions from S corporations and accumulated adjustments account

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corporation distributions are generally tax-free! But if the S corp has accumulated earnings from C corp years, the analysis gets complicated. Understanding AAA is essential.

sections.title: Simple Case: No C Corp History
sections.content:
**S corporations with NO accumulated E&P:**

Distributions are treated as:
1. Return of basis (tax-free) - reduces stock basis
2. Gain (when distributions exceed basis) - capital gain

**Example:**
• Shareholder basis: $50,000
• Distribution: $30,000
• Result: Tax-free, new basis = $20,000

sections.title: Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA)
sections.content:
**What is AAA?**
Corporate-level account tracking items that would affect shareholder basis.

**Increases:**
• Income items (not tax-exempt)
• Separately stated income

**Decreases:**
• Loss/deduction items
• Nondeductible expenses (not related to tax-exempt income)
• Distributions

**AAA can go negative** from losses (but distributions don't reduce AAA below zero).

sections.title: S Corporations WITH Accumulated E&P
sections.content:
**Distribution Ordering:**

1. First: From AAA (tax-free return of S corp earnings)
2. Then: From accumulated E&P (taxable dividend)
3. Then: From remaining AAA/basis (tax-free)
4. Finally: Excess over basis = capital gain

**Why it matters:**
Old E&P from C corp years can create taxable dividends!

**Election available:** Treat distribution as from E&P first (rare, but useful if shareholder has capital losses to offset).

sections.title: Comparison: AAA vs. Basis
rows: Cannot go below zero from distributions

sections.title: Other Tracked Accounts
sections.content:
**OAA (Other Adjustments Account):**
• Tax-exempt income
• Related nondeductible expenses
• Distributed only after AAA and E&P

**PTI (Previously Taxed Income):**
• Pre-1983 S corporation earnings
• Rarely encountered now

**Accumulated E&P:**
• From prior C corporation years
• Never increased by S corp operations

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: No E&P = simple: distributions reduce basis, then capital gain
With E&P: AAA first (tax-free), then E&P (dividend), then basis
AAA tracks S corp earnings at corporate level
AAA can go negative from losses
Distributions cannot reduce AAA below zero
Tax-exempt income increases basis but not AAA (increases OAA)

knowledgeCheck.question: An S corporation with accumulated E&P has an AAA of $20,000 and accumulated E&P of $15,000. A shareholder receives a distribution of $40,000. What is the amount of the distribution that is treated as a taxable dividend?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The distribution is first sourced from the AAA ($20,000), then from accumulated E&P ($15,000), which is treated as a taxable dividend. The remaining $5,000 is treated as a return of basis.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the order of distribution: AAA, then E&P, then basis.

reveal.question: List three items that INCREASE the Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA).
reveal.answer:
1. Income items (excluding tax-exempt income)
2. Separately stated income items
3. Excess of depletion over basis in property subject to depletion

sections.title: S Corp Distributions: No E&P vs. With E&P

rows.label: First Source of Distribution
values: Return of Basis
AAA

rows.label: Taxable Treatment if Distribution Exceeds Basis/AAA
values: Capital Gain
Taxable Dividend (from E&P)

rows.label: Impact on AAA
values: N/A (no AAA)
Decreases AAA

title: S Corporation Termination
description: Understand events that terminate S corporation election

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Losing S corporation status can be costly! Understanding termination events helps prevent inadvertent terminations and plan for controlled conversions when appropriate.

sections.title: Termination Events
content: Voluntary revocation by shareholders
Excess passive investment income (3 consecutive years with C corp E&P)
Ineligible shareholder (NRA, corporation, certain trusts)
More than 100 shareholders
Creation of second class of stock
Becoming ineligible corporation type

sections.title: Voluntary Revocation
sections.content:
**How to Revoke:**
Written consent by shareholders holding > 50% of shares.

**Effective Date:**
• On or before 15th day of 3rd month = effective January 1
• After = effective following year
• Can specify future effective date

**Example:**
• Revocation filed March 10, 2024 → Effective January 1, 2024
• Revocation filed March 20, 2024 → Effective January 1, 2025

sections.title: Passive Investment Income Termination
sections.content:
**Three Consecutive Years Rule:**

If for 3+ consecutive years:
• S corp has accumulated C corp E&P, AND
• Passive investment income > 25% of gross receipts

**Result:** S election terminates at start of year 4.

**Passive Investment Income:**
• Royalties, rents, dividends, interest
• NOT: Gains from active business

**Also:** Corporate-level tax on excess passive income if E&P exists.

sections.title: Inadvertent Termination Relief
sections.content:
**IRS Can Waive Termination If:**

• Termination was inadvertent
• Steps taken to correct (within reasonable time)
• Corporation and shareholders agree to IRS terms

**Common inadvertent terminations:**
• Stock transferred to ineligible shareholder
• Debt instrument treated as second class of stock
• Missing Form 2553

**Apply for relief:** Private letter ruling request

sections.title: Re-Election After Termination
sections.content:
**5-Year Waiting Period:**

After termination, cannot re-elect S status for 5 years without IRS consent.

**Exceptions:**
• IRS can waive waiting period
• Common if termination was inadvertent and quickly corrected
• Change of control may make waiver easier

**Plan ahead:** If contemplating conversion, consider timing and consequences.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Revocation requires > 50% shareholder consent
Timing of revocation affects effective date
Passive income can terminate election (3 years with E&P)
Ineligible shareholders cause immediate termination
Inadvertent termination relief available through IRS
5-year waiting period before re-election

knowledgeCheck.question: An S corporation's shareholders, holding 60% of the outstanding shares, decide on April 1, 2024, to revoke the S election. They file the revocation with the IRS on April 10, 2024. Assuming they do not specify a future date, when will the S corporation election terminate?
options: January 1, 2024
April 10, 2024
January 1, 2025
April 1, 2024
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Because the revocation was filed after the 15th day of the 3rd month (March 15th), the termination is effective January 1 of the following year (2025), unless a specific future date is indicated in the revocation filing.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the deadline for filing and the impact on the effective date when no future date is specified.

reveal.question: What two conditions must be met for the S election to be terminated due to excessive passive investment income?
reveal.answer: 1. The S corporation has accumulated C corporation earnings and profits (E&P). 2. Passive investment income exceeds 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive years.

knowledgeCheck.question: For three consecutive years, an S corporation with accumulated C corporation E&P has passive investment income exceeding 25% of its gross receipts. In year 4, the passive investment income drops below 25% of gross receipts. When does the S election terminate?
options: At the end of year 3.
At the beginning of year 4.
The S election does not terminate because the passive income test failed in year 4.
At the end of year 4.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The S election terminates at the *start* of year 4. The fact that passive income drops below 25% in year 4 is irrelevant; the termination event has already occurred due to the three consecutive years of exceeding the limit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The termination happens at the *start* of the year after the three-year period.

title: Guaranteed Payments
description: Understand payments to partners for services and capital use

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Guaranteed payments are a unique hybrid - compensation to partners that doesn't depend on partnership profit. Understanding their treatment for both the partnership and partner is essential!

sections.title: What Are Guaranteed Payments?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Payments to a partner for services or use of capital determined without regard to partnership income.

**Key Characteristics:**
• Fixed amount (not dependent on profits)
• Paid for services or capital use
• Partner is NOT treated as employee
• Taxable in partner's tax year that includes partnership year-end

**Common Examples:**
• Monthly management fee to managing partner
• Interest-like return on partner's capital account

sections.title: Partnership Calculation
sections.content:
**Order of Operations:**

1. Calculate partnership income/loss BEFORE guaranteed payments
2. Deduct guaranteed payments
3. Remaining income/loss allocated per agreement

**Example:**
Partnership has $100,000 income before guaranteed payments.
Partner A receives $30,000 guaranteed payment.
• Partnership ordinary income: $70,000 (to allocate)
• Partner A also includes $30,000 guaranteed payment
• Total allocated: $100,000

sections.title: ⚠️ Guaranteed Payment vs. Distribution
sections.content:
**Don't confuse them!**

**Guaranteed Payment:**
• Fixed, regardless of profit
• Deductible by partnership
• Ordinary income to recipient
• Subject to SE tax

**Distribution:**
• Share of profits (variable)
• Not deductible
• Generally not taxable (return of basis)
• Not subject to SE tax

**Exam frequently tests this distinction!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Guaranteed payments: fixed amount for services/capital
Deductible by partnership, ordinary income to partner
Subject to self-employment tax
Reported on Schedule K-1
Partner is NOT an employee - no W-2
Distributions are different: not deductible, not income

knowledgeCheck.question: Partnership XYZ has gross income of $200,000 before any guaranteed payments. Partner A receives a guaranteed payment of $60,000 for services rendered. The partnership agreement states that any remaining profit or loss is allocated 50% to Partner A and 50% to Partner B. What is the amount of income Partner A will report from the partnership, before considering any other items?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, deduct the guaranteed payment from the partnership income: $200,000 - $60,000 = $140,000. This remaining income is then allocated according to the partnership agreement. Partner A's share is 50% of $140,000 = $70,000.  The total income Partner A reports is the guaranteed payment plus their share of the remaining income: $60,000 + $70,000 = $130,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to account for both the guaranteed payment and the allocation of partnership income.

reveal.question: True or False: Guaranteed payments are dependent on partnership profits.
reveal.answer: False. Guaranteed payments are determined WITHOUT regard to partnership income.

sections.title: Guaranteed Payments vs. Partner's Share of Profits

rows.label: Payment Basis
values: Fixed amount, regardless of partnership profit
Based on the partnership's net income and allocation agreement

rows.label: Deduction from Partnership Income
values: Deductible by the partnership before profit allocation
Not deductible; it's an allocation of existing profit

rows.label: Purpose
values: Compensation for services or use of capital
Distribution of partnership earnings

title: Partnership Liabilities and Basis
description: Understand how partnership debt affects partner basis

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnership liabilities increase partner basis! This is a major advantage of partnerships over S corps. Understanding recourse vs. nonrecourse allocation rules is essential for SEE success.

sections.title: Why Liabilities Matter
sections.content:
**Liabilities increase partner basis:**

• More basis = ability to deduct more losses
• Distributions in excess of basis = capital gain
• At-risk rules affected by liability type

**Key partnership advantage:**
Unlike S corps, partnership debt CAN increase basis.

sections.title: Recourse vs. Nonrecourse
rows: Partner bears economic risk of loss
Real estate financing from unrelated parties

sections.title: Economic Risk of Loss
sections.content:
**Constructive Liquidation Test:**

To determine who bears economic risk:
1. Assume all assets become worthless
2. Partnership liquidates
3. Who must pay creditors?

**Risk bearers:**
• Partners with personal liability (general partners)
• Partners who guarantee debt
• Partners with capital contributions subject to loss

**Limited partners:** Usually no economic risk on general liabilities

sections.title: Allocation Examples
sections.content:
**Recourse Debt Example:**
Two general partners, 50/50, jointly liable on $100K loan.
• Each partner: $50,000 basis increase

**Nonrecourse Debt Example:**
Partnership borrows $200K (nonrecourse secured by equipment).
• Allocated by profit-sharing ratios
• 60/40 split: Partner A = $120K, Partner B = $80K

**Mixed:** If partner guarantees portion, that portion is recourse to them.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnership liabilities increase partner basis
Recourse debt: allocated to partners bearing economic risk
Nonrecourse debt: allocated by profit-sharing percentage
Qualified nonrecourse: at-risk for real estate debt
This is a major advantage over S corporations
Guarantee of debt = economic risk = recourse to guarantor

knowledgeCheck.question: Partnership ABC has two general partners, Alice and Bob, who share profits and losses equally. The partnership takes out a $200,000 recourse loan. Alice personally guarantees the entire loan. How much of the loan will be allocated to Alice for basis purposes?
options: It depends on the partnership agreement.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Because Alice personally guarantees the entire loan, she bears the economic risk of loss for the entire $200,000. Therefore, the full amount is allocated to her for basis purposes.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider who would be responsible if the partnership defaulted on the loan.

reveal.question: Describe the constructive liquidation test used to determine economic risk of loss.
reveal.answer: The constructive liquidation test involves assuming all partnership assets are worthless, the partnership liquidates, and then determining which partners would be obligated to pay creditors.

rows.label: Partner Liability
values: Partners are personally liable.
Partners are generally not personally liable (secured by assets).

rows.label: Allocation Basis
values: Economic risk of loss (who would have to pay if the partnership couldn't).
Profit-sharing ratios.

rows.label: Basis Increase
values: Increases partner's basis.
Increases partner's basis.

knowledgeCheck.question: A partnership agreement allocates profits and losses 70% to Partner X and 30% to Partner Y. The partnership obtains a $500,000 nonrecourse loan secured by partnership property. How much of the loan is allocated to Partner Y for basis purposes?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Nonrecourse debt is allocated according to the profit-sharing ratios. Partner Y's share is 30% of $500,000, which equals $150,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the allocation method for nonrecourse debt.

title: Partnership Special Allocations
description: Understand substantive economic effect requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Partnerships have flexibility to allocate items disproportionately! But the IRS has rules to prevent tax-motivated allocations with no economic substance. Understand when special allocations work.

sections.title: What Are Special Allocations?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Allocations of specific items (income, deduction, credit) in ratios different from general profit/loss sharing.

**Examples:**
• Depreciation allocated to high-bracket partner
• Capital gains allocated to partner with losses
• Rental income allocated differently than other income

**Requirement:** Must have substantial economic effect!

sections.title: Substantial Economic Effect
sections.content:
**Two-Part Test:**

**1. Economic Effect (allocations must affect economics):**
• Capital accounts maintained per regulations
• Liquidating distributions follow capital accounts
• Deficit restoration obligation OR qualified income offset

**2. Substantiality (cannot be tax-motivated only):**
• Must be reasonable possibility allocation will substantially affect dollar amounts apart from tax consequences
• Test at time allocation becomes part of agreement

sections.title: Safe Harbor Rules
sections.content:
**Primary Safe Harbor:**

1. Capital accounts maintained properly
2. On liquidation, partner receives positive capital or nothing
3. Partner with deficit must restore it OR
   - Qualified income offset (future income offsets deficit)

**If safe harbor met:** IRS respects the allocation.

**If not met:** Reallocate according to partner's interest in the partnership.

sections.title: Allocations That Fail
sections.content:
**Shifting Allocations:**
Allocations that shift tax consequences among partners with no business purpose.

**Transitory Allocations:**
• Allocation in Year 1 followed by offsetting allocation in Year 2
• Net economic effect is zero
• Only purpose is tax reduction

**Example of failure:**
Allocate $100K depreciation to Partner A in Year 1.
Allocate $100K gain to Partner A in Year 5 when property sold.
Net economic effect = zero. Fails substantiality.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Special allocations allow different ratios for different items
Must have substantial economic effect
Capital accounts must follow special rules
Liquidating distributions must follow capital accounts
Tax-only allocations will be reallocated by IRS
Shifting and transitory allocations fail substantiality

sections.title: Knowledge Check: Special Allocations and Economic Effect

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for an allocation to have economic effect under the substantial economic effect test?
options: Capital accounts must be maintained according to the regulations.
Liquidating distributions must be made in accordance with positive capital account balances.
The partnership agreement must provide for special allocations.
A partner with a deficit in their capital account following liquidation must have a deficit restoration obligation or qualified income offset.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The existence of special allocations is the premise of the question, not a requirement for economic effect. The economic effect test focuses on how allocations impact the partners' capital accounts and their rights upon liquidation. The other options are direct requirements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what ensures the allocations truly affect the partners' economic positions.

reveal.question: List the three requirements of the Primary Safe Harbor under the substantial economic effect regulations.
reveal.answer:
1. Capital accounts are maintained properly according to the regulations.
2. Upon liquidation, a partner either receives assets equal to their positive capital account balance or receives nothing.
3. A partner with a deficit in their capital account following liquidation has an unconditional obligation to restore the deficit (Deficit Restoration Obligation) or the partnership agreement contains a Qualified Income Offset (QIO) provision.

sections.title: Comparison: Deficit Restoration Obligation vs. Qualified Income Offset

rows.label: Obligation
values: Partner has an unconditional obligation to restore a deficit capital account upon liquidation.

The partnership agreement contains a QIO provision, which requires that if a partner unexpectedly receives an allocation or distribution that creates or increases a deficit capital account, the partner must be allocated income and gain in an amount and manner sufficient to eliminate the deficit as quickly as possible.

rows.label: Timing of Offset
values: Deficit is restored generally upon liquidation.
Deficit is offset by future income and gain, as quickly as possible.

rows.label: Impact on Flexibility
values: Provides more flexibility in initial allocations, as the deficit will be restored later.
Limits the types of allocations that can create or increase a deficit capital account.

title: Partnership Hot Assets
description: Understand ordinary income treatment on partnership interest sales

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When a partnership interest is sold, not all gain is capital gain! Hot assets cause ordinary income recognition. This is heavily tested on SEE. Know Section 751!

sections.title: What Are Hot Assets?
sections.content:
**Section 751 Assets:**

**1. Unrealized Receivables:**
• Accounts receivable (cash method)
• Recapture (Section 1245/1250)
• Rights to payment for services/goods

**2. Inventory Items:**
• Regular inventory
• Property held primarily for sale
• Property that would NOT be capital asset if sold

**Why "Hot":**
Convert capital gain to ordinary income!

sections.title: Sale of Partnership Interest
sections.content:
**General Rule:**
Partnership interest is capital asset → capital gain/loss on sale.

**Exception - Section 751:**
Portion of gain attributable to hot assets = ordinary income.

**Calculation:**
1. Determine FMV of selling partner's share of hot assets
2. Compare to basis in those assets
3. Difference = ordinary income
4. Remaining gain/loss = capital

sections.title: Example Calculation
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Partner A sells 25% interest for $100,000
• A's outside basis: $60,000
• Total gain: $40,000
• Partnership unrealized receivables: $80,000 (FMV)
• A's basis in receivables: $0 (cash method)

**Section 751 Analysis:**
• A's share of receivables: $20,000 (25%)
• A's basis: $0
• Ordinary income: $20,000

• Remaining gain: $40,000 - $20,000 = $20,000
• Character: Capital gain

**Result:** $20,000 ordinary + $20,000 capital gain

sections.title: Section 751(b) Distributions
sections.content:
**Disproportionate Distributions:**

If distribution shifts hot assets between partners:
• Partner receiving more than share of hot assets → ordinary income
• Treated as disguised sale

**Example:**
Partner receives all inventory, other partners receive cash.
Excess inventory distributed = ordinary income to recipient.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Hot assets: unrealized receivables and inventory
Section 751 converts capital gain to ordinary income
Must calculate ordinary income portion separately
Recapture items are unrealized receivables
Disproportionate distributions also trigger 751
Remaining gain after hot assets = capital gain

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following assets would NOT be considered a 'hot asset' under Section 751 for partnership taxation purposes?
options: Accounts receivable of a cash-basis partnership
Inventory held by the partnership for sale to customers
Section 1245 recapture potential on depreciable property
Land held by the partnership as an investment, treated as a capital asset
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Land held purely as an investment, treated as a capital asset, does not fall under the definition of 'hot assets.' Hot assets include unrealized receivables and inventory items that would generate ordinary income if sold.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which assets would generate ordinary income versus capital gains if sold by the partnership.

reveal.question: How do you determine the amount of ordinary income recognized by a partner selling their partnership interest that is attributable to hot assets?
reveal.answer:
1. Determine the fair market value (FMV) of the selling partner's share of the partnership's hot assets.
2. Determine the partner's basis in their share of those hot assets.
3. The difference between the FMV and the basis is the ordinary income recognized.

knowledgeCheck.question: A partnership has two assets: $50,000 cash and $50,000 in inventory. Partners A and B each have a 50% interest. If Partner A receives a distribution of all the inventory, while Partner B receives all the cash, what is the tax consequence for Partner A?
options: Partner A recognizes capital gain equal to the FMV of the inventory.
Partner A recognizes ordinary income equal to the FMV of the inventory.
Partner A is treated as having purchased the excess inventory from the other partners, resulting in ordinary income.
Partner A recognizes no gain or loss because the distribution is within their partnership interest.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: This is a disproportionate distribution under Section 751(b). Partner A is treated as having purchased the excess inventory from the other partners, resulting in ordinary income equal to the difference between the FMV of the excess inventory received and what Partner A's basis would be if it was proportionate.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about how disproportionate distributions of hot assets are treated under Section 751(b).

title: Partnership Terminations
description: Understand when a partnership terminates for tax purposes

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: A partnership terminates for tax purposes under specific circumstances. Understanding these rules helps plan ownership changes and avoid unintended consequences.

sections.title: When Partnership Terminates
sections.content:
**Current Rule (post-TCJA):**

A partnership terminates when:

1. No part of business continues to be carried on in a partnership, OR

2. Partnership merges or consolidates

**What no longer causes termination:**
• Sale or exchange of 50%+ of interests within 12 months (old rule repealed by TCJA)

sections.title: Merger Rules
sections.content:
**Which Partnership Continues?**

• The resulting partnership is treated as continuation of:
  - The partnership whose partners own > 50% of resulting partnership

• Other merging partnerships terminate

**Example:**
Partnership A merges into Partnership B.
If A's partners own 60% of B afterward, A continues, B terminates.
If B's partners own 60%, B continues, A terminates.

sections.title: Division of Partnership
sections.content:
**When Partnership Divides:**

• Resulting partnership that has continuing partner interests continues the prior partnership

• New partnership treated as newly formed

**Considerations:**
• Basis and holding periods may carry over
• May need new EIN for new partnership
• Section 704(c) considerations

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Partnership terminates when business ceases or only 1 partner
50%+ ownership change NO longer causes termination (TCJA)
Merger: partnership with > 50% continuing partners survives
Other merging partnerships terminate
Conversion to LLC generally is not a termination
Division creates one continuing and one new partnership

knowledgeCheck.question: Partnership XYZ merges into Partnership ABC. After the merger, the partners of XYZ own 40% of the resulting partnership, while the partners of ABC own 60%. Which of the following is true?
options: Partnership XYZ continues, and Partnership ABC terminates.
Partnership ABC continues, and Partnership XYZ terminates.
Both partnerships terminate, and a new partnership is formed.
Both partnerships continue, with the resulting entity being a continuation of both.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: According to the merger rules, the resulting partnership is treated as a continuation of the partnership whose partners own > 50% of the resulting partnership. In this case, ABC's partners own 60%, so ABC continues, and XYZ terminates.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which partnership's owners have the majority stake in the new entity.

reveal.question: What are the two scenarios that cause a partnership to terminate under the current (post-TCJA) rules?
reveal.answer:
1. No part of the business continues to be carried on in a partnership.
2. The partnership merges or consolidates.

rows.label: Definition
values: Two or more partnerships combine into one.
A partnership splits into two or more partnerships.

rows.label: Continuation Rule
values: The partnership whose partners own > 50% of the resulting partnership continues.
Resulting partnership with partners having > 50% interest in prior partnership continues it.

rows.label: New Partnership Formation
values: Possible, but not standard
New partnership is treated as newly formed

title: S Corporation Shareholder Basis
description: Master the calculation of S corp shareholder stock basis

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corporation basis rules determine if losses are deductible and distributions are taxable. Unlike partnerships, S corp debt does NOT increase basis unless the shareholder lends money directly!

sections.title: Stock Basis Formula
sections.content:
**Annual Calculation:**

Starting Basis
+ Income items (ordinary and separately stated)
+ Tax-exempt income
- Nondeductible expenses
- Distributions
- Losses and deductions (limited to basis)
= Ending Basis

**Order matters!**
Distributions reduce basis before losses.

sections.title: Debt Basis
sections.content:
**S Corp Debt Basis:**

Shareholder gets basis for debt ONLY if:
• Shareholder personally loans money to S corp
• NOT loans from banks guaranteed by shareholder
• NOT loans from related parties

**Debt basis used:**
• After stock basis exhausted
• For losses only (not distributions)

**Restoration:**
When income exceeds losses, debt basis restored first, then stock basis increases.

sections.title: Loss Limitation Ordering
sections.content:
**Three Hurdles:**

1. **Stock and Debt Basis Limitation**
   - First against stock basis
   - Then against debt basis

2. **At-Risk Limitation**
   - Amount shareholder can actually lose

3. **Passive Activity Loss Rules**
   - If passive, limited to passive income

**Suspended losses carry forward indefinitely (until basis available or disposition).**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Guaranteed Loans
sections.content:
**Guaranteeing corporate debt does NOT create basis!**

**Common mistake:**
S corp borrows $100K from bank.
Shareholder personally guarantees the loan.
Shareholder thinks they have $100K basis.

**Reality:**
No basis from guarantee.
Must actually advance funds to create debt basis.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: S corp basis from: contributions + income + direct loans
Entity-level debt does NOT increase shareholder basis
Debt basis only from actual loans to corporation
Distributions reduce basis before losses
Guarantees do NOT create basis
Losses limited to stock + debt basis (then at-risk, then PAL)

knowledgeCheck.question: John, a shareholder in an S corporation, has a beginning stock basis of $10,000. During the year, the S corporation allocated him $5,000 in ordinary income, $2,000 in tax-exempt income, and $1,000 in nondeductible expenses. He also received a distribution of $4,000. What is John's ending stock basis before considering any losses?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ending basis is calculated as follows: $10,000 (Starting Basis) + $5,000 (Ordinary Income) + $2,000 (Tax-Exempt Income) - $1,000 (Nondeductible Expenses) - $4,000 (Distributions) = $12,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the order matters. Income increases basis, while distributions and nondeductible expenses decrease it.

reveal.question: Under what circumstances does an S corporation shareholder receive debt basis?
reveal.answer: A shareholder receives debt basis only when they personally loan money directly to the S corporation. Loans from banks guaranteed by the shareholder, or loans from related parties, do not create debt basis.

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, an S corporation shareholder, has a stock basis of $2,000 and a debt basis of $1,000. The S corporation allocated her a loss of $4,000. Assuming she meets the at-risk and passive activity loss requirements, how much of the loss can Sarah deduct in the current year?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Sarah can deduct $3,000 of the loss. The loss is first applied against her stock basis ($2,000), reducing it to zero. Then, the remaining loss is applied against her debt basis ($1,000), reducing it to zero. The remaining $1,000 loss is suspended and carried forward.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to apply the loss first against stock basis and then against debt basis.

rows.label: Source
values: Initial investment, retained earnings, capital contributions
Direct loans made by the shareholder to the S corporation

rows.label: Order of Use
values: Used before debt basis
Used after stock basis is exhausted

rows.label: Impact of Distributions
values: Reduces stock basis
Does not reduce debt basis

rows.label: Use
values: For both losses and distributions
For losses only

title: S Corporation Built-In Gains Tax
description: Understand the BIG tax on C to S conversions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The BIG tax prevents C corps from converting to S status to avoid corporate-level tax on appreciated assets. Understanding this tax is critical for entity conversion planning!

sections.title: What is BIG Tax?
sections.content:
**Built-In Gains Tax:**

Corporate-level tax (21%) on gains that existed when:
• C corp converted to S corp, OR
• S corp acquired C corp assets (certain transactions)

**Purpose:**
Prevent avoidance of corporate-level tax by converting to S status before selling appreciated assets.

sections.title: Recognition Period
sections.content:
**5-Year Recognition Period:**

BIG tax applies to gains recognized within 5 years of S election.

**After 5 years:**
No BIG tax - gains pass through tax-free to shareholders.

**Planning opportunity:**
Wait 5 years after S election before selling highly appreciated C corp assets.

sections.title: Calculating BIG Tax
rows: Identify built-in gain (FMV - basis at conversion)
Net built-in gain = lesser of recognized or remaining built-in
Reduce by net operating losses carried from C corp years

sections.title: Example
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• C corp converts to S corp on 1/1/2024
• Asset A: Basis $50K, FMV $200K at conversion
• Built-in gain: $150K
• Year 2 (within recognition period): Sell for $220K

**Analysis:**
• Recognized gain: $170K ($220K - $50K)
• Built-in gain at conversion: $150K
• BIG tax applies to: $150K (lesser of recognized or built-in)
• BIG tax: $150K × 21% = $31,500

**Additional $20K gain passes through tax-free at corporate level.**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: BIG tax: 21% on gains that existed at C to S conversion
Recognition period: 5 years from S election
Only applies to gains recognized within recognition period
Limited to built-in gain amount at conversion
C corp NOLs can offset BIG tax
After 5 years, no BIG tax exposure

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corp converted from a C corporation to an S corporation on January 1, 2024. In October 2027, ABC Corp sells a building that it owned since 2020. At the time of the S election, the building's fair market value was $500,000 and its adjusted basis was $300,000. The building was sold in 2027 for $600,000. What is the amount of the built-in gain recognized in 2027?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The built-in gain is the appreciation that occurred before the S election. In this case, the built-in gain was $200,000 ($500,000 FMV - $300,000 basis). Since the sale occurred within the 5-year recognition period, and the gain realized ($600,000 - $300,000 = $300,000) is greater than the built-in gain, the built-in gain recognized is $200,000. It is important to recognize the property was sold within 5 years of the conversion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the fair market value and adjusted basis at the time of the S election. Also, remember the 5-year recognition period.

reveal.question: What is the general length of the recognition period for the Built-In Gains Tax?
reveal.answer: The recognition period is generally 5 years, beginning on the date of the S election. Gains recognized within this period are subject to the BIG tax if they existed at the time of the C to S conversion or acquisition of C corp assets.

sections.title: S Corp Conversion and BIG Tax

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the primary purpose of the Built-In Gains Tax?
options: To encourage C corporations to remain as C corporations.
To provide additional tax revenue to the IRS.
To prevent C corporations from avoiding corporate-level tax on appreciated assets by converting to S status before selling those assets.
To penalize S corporations for acquiring C corporation assets.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Built-In Gains Tax is designed to prevent tax avoidance. Without it, a C corporation could convert to an S corporation (which generally doesn't pay corporate-level tax) right before selling appreciated assets, thus avoiding the corporate-level tax. The BIG tax ensures that the appreciation that occurred while the company was a C corporation is taxed at the corporate level when the asset is sold within 5 years of the conversion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the tax implications of C corporations versus S corporations.

title: S Corporation Passive Income Tax
description: Understand the excess passive income tax and termination risk

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corps with C corp history face a passive income tax if they hold too much investment income! Worse, 3 consecutive years can terminate the S election. Know when this applies!

sections.title: When Does This Apply?
sections.content:
**Two Requirements:**

1. S corp has accumulated E&P from C corp years
   (E&P = Earnings & Profits - retained earnings from C corp)

2. Passive investment income > 25% of gross receipts

**If BOTH present:**
• Corporate-level tax on excess passive income
• 3 consecutive years = S election terminates!

sections.title: Passive Investment Income
content: Royalties
Rents (unless active real estate business)
Dividends
Interest (not from trade or business)
Annuities
Gains from stocks and securities sales

sections.title: Tax Calculation
sections.content:
**Excess Net Passive Income:**

(Net Passive Income) × (Passive Income - 25% of Gross Receipts) ÷ Passive Investment Income

**Tax Rate:** 21%

**Limitation:**
Cannot exceed taxable income for the year.

**Pass-through effect:**
Amount taxed at corporate level reduces shareholder pass-through.

sections.title: ⚠️ Three-Year Termination Rule
sections.content:
**If for 3 consecutive years:**
• S corp has C corp E&P, AND
• Passive income > 25% of gross receipts

**Consequence:**
S election terminates as of first day of 4th year.

**Solution:**
• Distribute C corp E&P (taxable dividend)
• Increase active business income
• Watch passive income levels carefully!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Tax applies only if S corp has C corp E&P
Passive income must exceed 25% of gross receipts
Tax rate: 21% on excess net passive income
3 consecutive years = S election terminates
Solution: distribute C corp E&P as dividend
Newly formed S corps don't have E&P (not applicable)

sections.title: Excess Passive Income Tax: Eligibility Check

knowledgeCheck.question: S Corp XYZ has $500,000 in gross receipts and $200,000 in passive investment income. It also has $50,000 in accumulated earnings and profits (E&P) from its prior C corp status. Is S Corp XYZ subject to the excess passive income tax?
options: Yes, because passive investment income exceeds 25% of gross receipts.
No, because the accumulated E&P is less than the passive investment income.
Yes, because both the passive investment income exceeds 25% of gross receipts and it has accumulated E&P from a prior C corp status.
No, because the passive income is less than 50% of gross receipts.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The S corporation is subject to the excess passive income tax because BOTH conditions are met: passive investment income ($200,000) exceeds 25% of gross receipts ($500,000 * 0.25 = $125,000), and the S corporation has accumulated E&P ($50,000) from a prior C corp status.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember, BOTH requirements must be met for the tax to apply.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating Excess Net Passive Income (ENPI)?
reveal.answer: (Net Passive Income) × (Passive Income - 25% of Gross Receipts) ÷ Passive Investment Income

knowledgeCheck.question: For how many consecutive years must an S corporation have excess passive income to trigger termination of the S election?
options: 2 years
3 years
4 years
5 years
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An S election is terminated if the corporation has excess passive income for three consecutive years.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about long-term consistency.

title: S Corporation Compensation Issues
description: Understand reasonable compensation requirements for S corp owners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: S corp owners who actively work must receive reasonable compensation! The IRS aggressively challenges low or zero salaries to avoid payroll taxes. This is an audit hot spot.

sections.title: The Issue
sections.content:
**Why owners underpay themselves:**

• Distributions avoid payroll tax (15.3%)
• Salary subject to payroll tax
• Temptation: Take all income as distributions

**IRS Response:**
• Require "reasonable compensation"
• Recharacterize distributions as wages
• Assess payroll taxes + penalties + interest

sections.title: What is Reasonable Compensation?
sections.content:
**Factors IRS Considers:**

• Training and experience
• Duties and responsibilities
• Time devoted to business
• Comparable salaries in similar businesses
• Compensation agreements with non-shareholders
• Use of corporate profits for personal expenses
• Business economic history

sections.title: Compensation Guidelines
rows: Salary from each where services rendered

sections.title: Consequences of Underpayment
sections.content:
**If IRS recharacterizes distributions as wages:**

• Back payroll taxes (employer + employee portions)
• Failure to deposit penalties
• Interest from original due dates
• Potential accuracy-related penalties (20%)
• Trust fund recovery penalty on responsible persons

**Example:**
$100K recharacterized × 15.3% = $15,300 payroll taxes
Plus penalties and interest!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Active S corp owners must receive reasonable compensation
Cannot pay zero salary and take all distributions
IRS compares to what similar businesses pay
Distributions in excess of salary are fine
Recharacterization = back payroll taxes + penalties
Document compensation decisions

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following factors is LEAST likely to be considered by the IRS when determining reasonable compensation for an S corporation shareholder-employee?
options: The shareholder-employee's training and experience.
Comparable salaries paid to employees in similar businesses.
The amount of S corporation distributions paid to the shareholder-employee.
The shareholder-employee's personal investment portfolio performance.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IRS focuses on factors related to the shareholder-employee's role and contribution to the business. Personal investment portfolio performance is not relevant to determining reasonable compensation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what directly contributes to the S corporation's success.

reveal.question: List at least three potential penalties the IRS can impose if they recharacterize S corporation distributions as wages.
reveal.answer:
1. Back payroll taxes (employer and employee portions).
2. Failure to deposit penalties.
3. Interest from original due dates.
4. Potential accuracy-related penalties (20%).
5. Trust fund recovery penalty on responsible persons.

knowledgeCheck.question: Why might an S corporation owner be tempted to take a majority of their compensation as distributions rather than salary?
options: Distributions are generally taxed at a lower rate than salaries.
Distributions are not subject to self-employment tax.
Distributions avoid payroll taxes (FICA and Medicare).
Distributions are not reported to the IRS.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Distributions are not subject to payroll taxes (FICA and Medicare), which can be a significant incentive for owners to underpay themselves in salary and overcompensate with distributions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the taxes applied to salary versus distributions.

title: Choosing Business Entity Type
description: Compare entity types and understand the selection factors

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Choosing the right entity type is one of the most important business decisions! Tax implications, liability protection, and operational flexibility all depend on this choice.

sections.title: When to Choose C Corporation
content: Planning to go public or seek venture capital
Retain significant earnings (21% rate < individual rates)
Significant fringe benefit needs (> 2% owners)
Foreign shareholders
More than 100 shareholders
Multiple classes of stock needed

sections.title: When to Choose S Corporation
content: Avoid double taxation
Pass through losses to shareholders
Reduce self-employment tax (distributions not subject)
Fewer than 100 shareholders
All shareholders are U.S. individuals or certain trusts
Single class of stock acceptable

sections.title: When to Choose Partnership/LLC
content: Maximum flexibility in allocations
Debt basis benefit important
Special allocations needed
More than 100 owners
Foreign owners
Corporate owners
Multiple classes of interests needed

sections.title: QBI Deduction Consideration
sections.content:
**Section 199A - 20% QBI Deduction:**

• Available for pass-through entities (S corps, partnerships, sole props)
• NOT available for C corporations
• Phase-outs for specified service businesses (SSTB)
• Income limitations apply

**Can make pass-through more attractive than C corp in some cases!**

Effective rate on pass-through: 37% × 80% = 29.6% vs. 21% + shareholder tax

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: C corp: Entity-level tax, best for retention or public companies
S corp: Pass-through, reduces SE tax, ownership restrictions
Partnership/LLC: Maximum flexibility, debt basis, no owner limits
QBI deduction: 20% for pass-throughs, not C corps
Consider both tax and non-tax factors
Entity choice can be changed (with tax consequences)

knowledgeCheck.question: A client anticipates significant losses in the initial years of operation but intends to become highly profitable thereafter. They desire to personally utilize the early losses to offset other income. Which business entity type would be MOST advantageous from a tax perspective during the start-up phase?
options: C Corporation
S Corporation
Partnership/LLC
Sole Proprietorship
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Partnerships and LLCs offer pass-through taxation, allowing the owners to deduct the business's losses on their individual income tax returns. C corporations do not offer this pass-through benefit. S corporations pass through income and losses, but the client would need to meet the requirements of that structure.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the tax implications of losses and how they are treated under different entity structures.

reveal.question: What is the maximum number of shareholders permitted in an S Corporation?
reveal.answer: An S Corporation can have no more than 100 shareholders. All shareholders must generally be U.S. citizens or resident aliens.

sections.title: C Corporation vs. S Corporation: Key Tax Differences

rows.label: Taxation of Profits
values: Subject to double taxation (corporate level and shareholder level upon distribution)
Pass-through taxation (profits and losses are passed through to the shareholders' individual income tax returns)

rows.label: Shareholder Compensation
values: Shareholder-employees are typically paid wages subject to payroll taxes.
Shareholder-employees are typically paid wages subject to payroll taxes, but distributions are not subject to self-employment taxes.

rows.label: Capital Gains/Losses
values: Capital gains/losses are taxed at the corporate level.
Capital gains/losses are passed through to the shareholders and taxed at the individual level.

title: C Corporation Formation and Income
description: Understand corporate formation, income recognition, and tax rates

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: C corporations are the traditional corporate form with entity-level taxation. Understanding formation rules and the 21% flat rate is essential for tax planning and SEE success.

sections.title: Corporate Tax Rate
sections.content:
**Flat 21% Rate on Taxable Income**

The TCJA replaced graduated rates with a single 21% rate.

**Key Points:**
• No personal service corporation penalty rate
• No accumulated earnings tax rate change
• Alternative minimum tax eliminated for corps

**Double Taxation:**
Corporate income taxed at 21%
+ Dividends taxed to shareholders at 0%/15%/20%
= Effective combined rate can exceed 35%

sections.title: Section 351: Tax-Free Formation
sections.content:
**Requirements for Nonrecognition:**

1. **Transfer of property** (not services)
2. **Solely in exchange for stock**
3. **Transferors control corporation immediately after** (≥80%)

**Control:** 80% of voting AND 80% of each class of nonvoting

**Result if met:**
• No gain/loss to transferors
• Corporation takes carryover basis
• Stock basis = basis of property transferred (minus boot + gain recognized)

sections.title: Exceptions to Section 351
sections.content:
**Boot Received:**
If cash or property other than stock is received:
• Gain recognized to extent of boot (not loss)
• Gain character depends on property transferred

**Services:**
• Stock for services = ordinary income to recipient
• The service provider doesn't count toward 80% control test

**Liability Assumption:**
Generally doesn't trigger gain unless:
• Tax avoidance purpose, or
• Liabilities exceed basis of ALL transferred property

sections.title: Corporate Income Recognition
sections.content:
**Gross Income = Same as individuals (generally):**
• Business income
• Investment income
• Gains from property sales
• Other income

**Special Corporate Items:**
• Dividends received deduction (DRD)
• No preferential capital gains rates
• No above-the-line deductions
• Different charitable contribution limits

**Taxable Income:**
Gross income - Deductions = Taxable Income
Taxable Income × 21% = Tax

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: C corporations taxed at flat 21% rate
Section 351 allows tax-free formation with 80% control
Boot received triggers gain recognition
Stock for services = immediate ordinary income
DRD: 50%, 65%, or 100% based on ownership percentage
No preferential capital gains rates for corporations
AMT eliminated for corporations under TCJA

knowledgeCheck.question: John transfers land with a basis of $50,000 and a fair market value of $150,000 to XYZ Corporation in exchange for 75% of XYZ's stock and $25,000 cash. Mary simultaneously transfers equipment with a basis of $20,000 and a fair market value of $75,000 in exchange for 25% of XYZ's stock. What is John's recognized gain?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: John recognizes gain to the extent of the boot (cash) received. Since he received $25,000 cash, he recognizes a gain of $25,000. Even though the total gain on the transfer was $100,000 ($150,000 FMV - $50,000 Basis), the gain recognized is limited to the boot received. The control requirement is met because John and Mary together own 100% of the stock.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that gain is recognized only to the extent of the boot received.

reveal.question: What two conditions must be met to satisfy the 'control' requirement in a Section 351 transfer?
reveal.answer: The transferors must own at least 80% of the total combined voting power of all classes of stock entitled to vote, AND at least 80% of the total number of shares of all other classes of stock (nonvoting).

sections.title: Stock for Property vs. Stock for Services

rows.label: Tax Treatment to Transferor
values: No gain or loss recognized (generally, assuming Section 351 requirements are met)
Ordinary income recognized equal to the fair market value of the stock received

rows.label: Impact on 80% Control Test
values: Transferor is included in the control group if other requirements of Section 351 are met.
Transferor is NOT included in the control group.

title: Corporate Distributions and Dividends
description: Understand the rules for corporate distributions to shareholders

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Whether a corporate distribution is a taxable dividend, return of capital, or capital gain depends on Earnings & Profits (E&P). This is a crucial concept for C corporation taxation.

sections.title: E&P Overview
sections.content:
**Earnings & Profits (E&P):**
A tax concept measuring a corporation's ability to pay dividends.

**Similar to retained earnings but NOT identical:**
• Starts with taxable income
• Add back tax-exempt income
• Add back certain timing differences
• Subtract federal income tax paid
• Subtract non-deductible/non-capital expenses

**Two types:**
• Current E&P (this year's earnings)
• Accumulated E&P (prior years' cumulative)

sections.title: Distribution Ordering Rules
sections.content:
**How to Characterize a Distribution:**

**Step 1:** First from CURRENT E&P (pro-rata if multiple distributions)
→ Dividend

**Step 2:** Then from ACCUMULATED E&P
→ Dividend

**Step 3:** Return of capital (reduces stock basis)
→ Not taxable

**Step 4:** After basis reduced to zero
→ Capital gain

**Key:** Current E&P is allocated pro-rata to all distributions; accumulated is chronological.

sections.title: Example: Distribution Characterization
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Current E&P: $30,000
• Accumulated E&P: $20,000
• Total distribution: $75,000
• Shareholder basis: $10,000

**Analysis:**
• Dividend (current E&P): $30,000
• Dividend (accumulated E&P): $20,000
• Return of capital (reduces basis): $10,000
• Capital gain (excess): $15,000

**After distribution:**
Shareholder basis = $0

sections.title: Constructive Dividends
sections.content:
**What are Constructive Dividends?**
Distributions disguised as something else:

**Common Examples:**
• Below-market rent on corporate property to shareholder
• Above-market compensation to shareholder-employees
• Personal expenses paid by corporation
• Loans to shareholders with no repayment intent
• Bargain sales of property to shareholders

**Tax Treatment:**
• Dividend to shareholder (current + accumulated E&P)
• Often nondeductible to corporation
• Double whammy!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: E&P measures dividend-paying capacity
Distributions first from current E&P (pro-rata)
Then from accumulated E&P (chronological)
After E&P exhausted: return of capital, then capital gain
Constructive dividends can arise from disguised benefits
Qualified dividends taxed at preferential rates to shareholders

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corp. has taxable income of $200,000. It paid $50,000 in federal income taxes, received $10,000 in tax-exempt interest income, and had a $5,000 net capital loss that was not deductible. What is XYZ Corp.'s E&P?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: E&P starts with taxable income ($200,000). Add back tax-exempt interest ($10,000). Subtract federal income tax paid ($50,000). The non-deductible capital loss does not affect E&P. $200,000 + $10,000 - $50,000 = $160,000. However, the capital loss is not deductible in the calculation of taxable income so it must be added back (since it did not reduce taxable income). Therefore $200,000 + $10,000 - $50,000 = $160,000 + $50,000 = $210,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to adjust for tax-exempt income and federal income taxes paid. Consider how non-deductible losses impact taxable income and E&P.

reveal.question: List the four steps, in order, for characterizing a corporate distribution.
reveal.answer:
1. Current E&P (pro-rata if multiple distributions)
2. Accumulated E&P
3. Return of Capital (reduces stock basis)
4. Capital Gain

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios is MOST likely to be considered a constructive dividend?
options: A corporation pays its shareholder-employee a salary comparable to similar positions in the industry.
A corporation provides its shareholder-employee with a company car for business use.
A corporation sells property to a shareholder at a price significantly below fair market value.
A corporation makes a pro-rata cash distribution to all shareholders based on their stock ownership.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Selling property below fair market value is a common example of a constructive dividend. The difference between the fair market value and the sale price is essentially a distribution to the shareholder. The other options are less likely to be considered constructive dividends, assuming they are reasonable and properly documented.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which scenario provides a benefit to the shareholder that is not available to non-shareholders and is disguised as something else.

rows.label: Purpose
values: Measures a corporation's ability to pay taxable dividends.
Represents the cumulative net income of a corporation less dividends paid out.

rows.label: Calculation Basis
values: Starts with taxable income, adjusted for specific items (e.g., tax-exempt income, federal income tax).
Based on accounting principles (GAAP).

rows.label: Tax Significance
values: Directly relevant for determining the taxability of corporate distributions.
Primarily a financial accounting concept, less direct tax impact.

title: Stock Redemptions
description: Understand when redemptions qualify for sale or exchange treatment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Stock redemptions can be treated as a sale (capital gain) or as a dividend (ordinary income). Understanding the tests for sale treatment can save shareholders significant taxes!

sections.title: Redemption Basics
sections.content:
**Stock Redemption:**
Corporation buys back (redeems) its own stock from a shareholder.

**Two Possible Treatments:**

**Sale Treatment (preferred):**
• Shareholder recognizes capital gain/loss
• Amount realized minus basis = gain/loss
• Basis is recovered

**Dividend Treatment:**
• Entire amount is dividend (to extent of E&P)
• No basis recovery
• Ordinary income (qualified dividend rates may apply)

sections.title: Section 302 Tests for Sale Treatment
rows: < 50% voting power AND < 80% of pre-redemption ownership
All shares redeemed (family attribution can be waived)

sections.title: Attribution Rules
sections.content:
**Constructive Ownership (Section 318):**

Shares owned by others are attributed to taxpayer:

**Family Attribution:**
• Spouse, children, grandchildren, parents
• NOT siblings!

**Entity Attribution:**
• Shareholder owns 50%+ of entity → attributed proportionally from entity
• Entity attributed ownership from 50%+ shareholders

**Option Attribution:**
• Option to acquire → treated as owner

sections.title: Waiver of Family Attribution
sections.content:
**Available for Complete Terminations:**

Family attribution can be waived if:
• All stock actually owned is redeemed
• No interest in corporation (other than creditor) for 10 years
• Does not acquire interest within 10 years (other than by bequest)
• Files agreement to notify IRS if reacquisition

**Result:** Can treat as complete termination even though family members still own stock.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Attribution Matters!
sections.content:
**Always check attribution:**

**Example:**
• Father owns 60%, Son owns 40%
• Father is redeemed completely
• Father is attributed Son's 40%
• Without waiver: NOT complete termination!
• Still owns 40% constructively

**Solution:** Waive family attribution if available.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Sale treatment = capital gain/loss with basis recovery
Dividend treatment = ordinary income, no basis recovery
Must meet Section 302 test for sale treatment
Attribution rules affect ownership percentage analysis
Family attribution: spouse, children, grandchildren, parents
Complete termination can waive family attribution
Substantially disproportionate: < 80% of previous percentage

knowledgeCheck.question: Shareholder Amy owns 100 of the 150 outstanding shares of XYZ Corporation. XYZ redeems 50 of Amy's shares. After the redemption, Amy owns 50 of the 100 outstanding shares. Amy's son, Ben, also owns 25 shares of XYZ. What percentage of the outstanding shares is Amy deemed to own after the redemption, considering attribution rules?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Amy actually owns 50/100 shares, which is 50%. Her son Ben owns 25 shares, which is 25% of the outstanding shares. Under family attribution, Ben's shares are attributed to Amy. Therefore, Amy is deemed to own 50% + 25% = 75% of the outstanding shares.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to consider both Amy's direct ownership and the shares attributed to her from family members.

reveal.question: List the family members whose stock ownership is attributed to a taxpayer under Section 318.
reveal.answer: Spouse, children, grandchildren, and parents. Siblings are NOT included in family attribution.

rows.label: Tax Impact
values: Capital gain/loss (potentially favorable rates)
Ordinary income (generally higher rates)

rows.label: Basis Recovery
values: Basis of redeemed shares is recovered (reduces gain)
No basis recovery; entire amount is treated as a dividend

rows.label: Shareholder Preference
values: Generally preferred by shareholders due to potentially lower tax rates and basis recovery
Generally less favorable to shareholders due to higher tax rates and no basis recovery

rows.label: Applicability
values: Requires meeting specific criteria under Section 302 (e.g., substantially disproportionate redemption, complete termination)
Applies if redemption does not meet the criteria for sale treatment

title: Corporate Liquidations
description: Understand the tax consequences of liquidating a corporation

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Liquidating a corporation has significant tax consequences for both the corporation and shareholders. Understanding these rules is essential for planning and executing exit strategies.

sections.title: General Rule: Section 331
sections.content:
**Shareholder Treatment (Non-Corporate):**

• Amounts received treated as payment for stock
• Gain/loss = Amount received - Stock basis
• Character: Capital gain/loss

**Corporation Treatment (Section 336):**
• Corporation recognizes gain/loss on distribution
• As if property sold at FMV
• Subject to depreciation recapture rules

sections.title: Exception: Section 332
sections.content:
**Parent-Subsidiary Liquidations:**

If parent owns 80%+ of subsidiary:

**Parent (Shareholder):**
• NO gain or loss recognized
• Takes carryover basis in assets
• Inherits subsidiary's tax attributes (NOLs, E&P)

**Subsidiary (Corporation):**
• Generally NO gain or loss recognized (Section 337)

**Requirements:**
• 80% ownership (voting + value)
• Complete liquidation
• Complete within 3 years or single plan

sections.title: Loss Limitations
sections.content:
**Losses NOT Recognized by Liquidating Corp:**

• Distributions to related persons (> 50%)
• Property distributed acquired in Section 351/contribution within 5 years with tax avoidance purpose
• Property subject to liability exceeding basis

**Anti-Stuffing Rules:**
Prevent contributions of loss property shortly before liquidation.

sections.title: Planning Considerations
sections.content:
**Before Liquidating:**

• Distribute appreciated property: Corporate-level gain + shareholder gain
• Built-in losses may be limited
• Consider S election before liquidation to avoid double tax on appreciation
• Parent-subsidiary liquidations (332) can defer gain
• E&P and tax attributes transfer to parent in 332

**Timing:** All distributions within same taxable year if possible.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Section 331: Shareholders recognize capital gain/loss on liquidation
Section 336: Corporation recognizes gain/loss on distribution
Section 332: Tax-free for 80%+ parent, carryover basis
Section 337: Subsidiary doesn't recognize gain/loss when liquidating into parent
Loss limitations apply to related party distributions
Minority shareholders always use Section 331 treatment

knowledgeCheck.question: Individual A owns 100 shares of XYZ Corp stock with a basis of $10,000. XYZ Corp liquidates under Section 331, and A receives assets valued at $15,000. If XYZ Corp liquidates under Section 332 and A is Parent Corp who owns 85% of XYZ Corp (Subsidiary Corp), what is the amount of gain/loss recognized by Individual A (S331) and Parent Corp (S332)?
options: Section 331: $5,000 Gain; Section 332: $0 Gain/Loss
Section 331: $0 Gain/Loss; Section 332: $5,000 Gain
Section 331: $5,000 Loss; Section 332: $0 Gain/Loss
Section 331: $0 Gain/Loss; Section 332: $5,000 Loss
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Under Section 331, the shareholder (Individual A) recognizes a capital gain of $5,000 ($15,000 - $10,000). Under Section 332, the parent corporation (Parent Corp) does not recognize any gain or loss on the liquidation of its 80%+ owned subsidiary (XYZ Corp).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the ownership percentage and the general rules for Section 331 and Section 332 liquidations.

reveal.question: What is the general rule for the corporation's treatment regarding gain or loss recognition under Section 336 during a corporate liquidation?
reveal.answer: Under Section 336, the liquidating corporation generally recognizes gain or loss on the distribution of its assets as if the property were sold at its fair market value (FMV).

rows.label: Ownership Threshold
values: > 50% ownership
N/A

rows.label: Time Limit
values: None
Within 5 years of liquidation

rows.label: Purpose
values: Loss cannot be recognized on distributions to related parties
Tax avoidance purpose

title: Accumulated Earnings Tax
description: Understand the penalty tax on excess corporate accumulations

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The accumulated earnings tax is a penalty tax designed to prevent C corporations from avoiding shareholder-level tax by accumulating earnings instead of distributing dividends.

sections.title: AET Basics
sections.content:
**Tax Rate:** 20% (same as top dividend rate)

**Applies to:**
• C corporations (not S corps)
• That accumulate earnings beyond reasonable business needs
• With intent to avoid shareholder-level tax

**Tax Base:**
Accumulated taxable income = Taxable income (with adjustments) - Dividends Paid Deduction - Accumulated Earnings Credit

sections.title: Accumulated Earnings Credit
sections.content:
**Minimum Credit:**
• $250,000 (most corporations)
• $150,000 (personal service corporations)

**What it allows:**
Corporation can accumulate up to credit amount without AET, even without specific plans.

**Reasonable Needs Above Credit:**
Must be justified by specific, definite, and feasible plans.

sections.title: Reasonable Business Needs
content: Business expansion and plant acquisition
Debt retirement
Working capital needs
Acquiring another business
Self-insurance reserves
Realistic contingency needs
Investment in subsidiaries
Product liability reserves

sections.title: NOT Reasonable Business Needs
content: Loans to shareholders
Personal investments unrelated to business
Vague or indefinite plans
Already adequate working capital
Accumulations for purposes already funded
Investments in property not used in business

sections.title: Avoiding AET
sections.content:
**Best Practices:**

• Document specific plans for retained earnings
• Corporate minutes should reflect decisions
• Pay reasonable dividends
• Maintain records of business needs
• Consider S election

**Documentation is key!**
IRS burden to prove tax avoidance purpose, but shifts to corporation with specific evidence.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AET: 20% penalty on excess accumulated earnings
Minimum credit: $250,000 ($150,000 for PSCs)
Must document specific, definite, feasible plans
Loans to shareholders suggest tax avoidance
S corporations exempt from AET
Dividends paid reduce exposure

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following corporations is MOST likely to be subject to the Accumulated Earnings Tax (AET)?
options: An S corporation that distributed 90% of its taxable income as dividends.
A C corporation that accumulated $300,000 in earnings and profits to fund a future, but not yet formally planned, expansion into a new market.
A C corporation with $100,000 in accumulated earnings and profits.
A C corporation that paid out 100% of its taxable income as dividends.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The AET applies to C corporations that accumulate earnings beyond reasonable business needs with the intent to avoid shareholder-level tax. Option B is the most likely candidate because it's a C corporation, has accumulated earnings exceeding the minimum credit ($250,000 for most corporations), and the expansion plan is not specific, definite, or feasible, suggesting a potential AET issue. S corporations are not subject to AET. A C corporation that distributes all of its income is also not subject to AET.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the type of entity and the purpose/documentation of the accumulated earnings.

reveal.question: What is the current tax rate for the Accumulated Earnings Tax (AET)?
reveal.answer: The Accumulated Earnings Tax is levied at a rate of 20%. This is the same as the top tax rate for dividends.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation, a manufacturing company, has accumulated $200,000 in earnings. It has no specific, definite, and feasible plans for expansion. What is the corporation's exposure to the Accumulated Earnings Tax?
options: The corporation is definitely subject to the Accumulated Earnings Tax.
The corporation is not subject to the Accumulated Earnings Tax due to the accumulated earnings credit.
The corporation is subject to the Accumulated Earnings Tax on the entire $200,000.
The corporation's exposure to the Accumulated Earnings Tax depends on the dividends paid during the tax year.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The corporation is not subject to the Accumulated Earnings Tax because it is still less than $250,000. The corporation can accumulate up to the credit amount without AET, even without specific plans.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the minimum accumulated earnings credit for most corporations.

title: Personal Holding Company Tax
description: Understand the PHC penalty tax and how to avoid it

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The PHC tax is another penalty tax on C corporations! It targets closely-held corporations that are primarily investment vehicles. Understanding the tests helps avoid this 20% penalty.

sections.title: PHC Definition
sections.content:
**Two-Part Test:**

**1. Stock Ownership Test:**
• More than 50% of stock owned by 5 or fewer individuals
• At any time during last half of year
• Apply attribution rules

**2. Income Test:**
• PHC income is 60%+ of adjusted ordinary gross income

**Both tests must be met** for PHC status.

sections.title: PHC Income
content: Dividends, interest, royalties, annuities
Rents (if < 50% of AOGI, subject to 10% test)
Mineral, oil, gas royalties (with exceptions)
Copyright royalties (with exceptions)
Personal service contracts (named person)
Amounts from estates/trusts

sections.title: Rent Exception
sections.content:
**Rents are NOT PHC Income if:**

• Rents are 50%+ of adjusted ordinary gross income, AND
• Other PHC income (dividends, etc.) is ≤ 10% of ordinary gross income, OR
  - Dividends paid equal or exceed other PHC income minus 10%

**Why it matters:**
Real estate corporations can often avoid PHC if primarily rental company.

sections.title: PHC Tax
sections.content:
**Tax Rate:** 20%

**Tax Base:**
Undistributed PHC Income = Taxable income (adjusted) - Federal income tax - Dividends paid

**How to Avoid Tax:**
• Distribute all PHC income as dividends
• Consent dividends (shareholder agreement without cash)
• Deficiency dividends (after IRS determination)

**Automatic if tests met** - no intent requirement unlike AET.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: PHC: 5 or fewer individuals own > 50% AND PHC income ≥ 60% of AOGI
PHC Tax: 20% on undistributed PHC income
PHC Income: Passive income (dividends, interest, royalties, etc.)
Rent exception: 50%+ rental income may avoid PHC status
Avoid by paying dividends on PHC income
No intent required (unlike AET)

knowledgeCheck.question: XYZ Corporation has five shareholders, with shareholders A, B, and C owning 20% each, and shareholders D and E owning 30% and 10% respectively. During the last half of the tax year, more than 50% of the company's stock was owned, directly or indirectly, by these 5 individuals. XYZ's adjusted ordinary gross income (AOGI) is $500,000, and its PHC income is $320,000. Is XYZ Corporation considered a Personal Holding Company (PHC)?
options: Yes, because both the stock ownership and income tests are met.
No, because the stock ownership test is not met.
No, because the income test is not met.
It cannot be determined without knowing the attribution rules.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The stock ownership test is met because more than 50% of the stock is owned by 5 or fewer individuals. The income test is met because PHC income ($320,000) is more than 60% of AOGI ($500,000 x 60% = $300,000). Therefore, XYZ is a PHC.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate 60% of the adjusted ordinary gross income. Does the PHC income exceed that amount?

reveal.question: What are the two conditions that must be met for rents to NOT be considered PHC income?
reveal.answer: 1. Rents are 50% or more of the adjusted ordinary gross income, AND 2. Other PHC income (dividends, etc.) is 10% or less of ordinary gross income, OR dividends paid equal or exceed other PHC income minus 10% of ordinary gross income.

knowledgeCheck.question: ABC Corporation has adjusted ordinary gross income of $800,000. Rents are $450,000. Dividend income is $60,000. Is ABC Corporation subject to the Personal Holding Company tax?
options: Yes, because rents are less than 50% of the adjusted ordinary gross income.
No, because rents are more than 50% of the adjusted ordinary gross income.
Yes, because the dividend income exceeds 10% of the ordinary gross income.
No, because the dividend income is less than 10% of the adjusted ordinary gross income.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Rents of $450,000 are less than 50% of AOGI ($800,000 * 0.5 = $400,000). Therefore, the rent exception is not met, and the company may be subject to PHC tax if the stock ownership test is also met.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Calculate 50% of the AOGI. Is the rent amount higher or lower?

title: Related Party Transactions
description: Understand special rules for related party transactions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Transactions between related parties get extra scrutiny! Loss disallowance, expense deferral, and ordinary income treatment are common consequences. Know the related party rules!

sections.title: Who Are Related Parties?
content: Family members (siblings, spouse, ancestors, lineal descendants)
Individual and corporation (> 50% ownership directly/indirectly)
Two corporations in same controlled group
Grantor and fiduciary of trust
Fiduciaries of two trusts with same grantor
Corporation and partnership with > 50% common ownership
Two partnerships with > 50% common ownership

sections.title: Section 267(a)(1): Loss Disallowance
sections.content:
**Losses on Sale to Related Party:**

Loss is disallowed when selling property to related party.

**What happens to the loss?**
