If a taxpayer shows a profit in at least 2 out of 7 consecutive tax years in an activity involving the racing or breeding of horses, the IRS presumes the activity is engaged in for profit, shifting the burden of proof to the IRS to demonstrate otherwise. This differs from the general rule of 3 out of 5 years.

rows.label: Profit Motive
values: Primary purpose is to generate profit
Lacks a genuine profit motive; primarily for recreation or pleasure

rows.label: Loss Deductibility
values: Losses are deductible against other income (subject to limitations)
Losses are NOT deductible (except for certain itemized deductions unrelated to the hobby)

rows.label: Income Taxability
values: Income is taxable
Income is taxable

rows.label: Section 183 Application
values: Section 183 generally doesn't apply if a genuine profit motive exists.
Section 183 applies to determine if the activity is not engaged in for profit.

title: Like-Kind Exchanges
description: Understand Section 1031 exchanges for real property

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Section 1031 allows complete deferral of gain on real property exchanges! This powerful tool lets investors grow wealth tax-free. After TCJA, only real property qualifies.

sections.title: Current Law (Post-TCJA)
sections.content:
**Only REAL PROPERTY qualifies:**

• Commercial buildings
• Apartments/rental houses
• Land
• Office buildings
• Industrial property

**No longer qualifies:**
• Personal property (equipment, vehicles)
• Artwork, collectibles
• Livestock
• Inventory

sections.title: Requirements
content: Both properties held for investment or business use
Properties are like-kind (real property for real property)
Must identify replacement within 45 days
Must close within 180 days (or tax return due date if earlier)
Qualified intermediary holds proceeds
Cannot receive cash or other property before exchange

sections.title: Timeline
sections.content:
**Day 0:** Close on relinquished property (sale)

**Day 45:** Deadline to identify replacement properties
• Can identify up to 3 properties (any value), OR
• Any number if total FMV ≤ 200% of relinquished

**Day 180:** Deadline to close on replacement property
• Or tax return due date, whichever is earlier
• Get extension if needed!

sections.title: Boot and Basis
sections.content:
**Boot = Taxable:**
• Cash received in exchange
• Property other than real estate
• Debt relief in excess of debt assumed

**Gain recognized = Lesser of:**
• Realized gain, or
• Boot received

**Basis in new property:**
FMV of new property - deferred gain

**Example:**
Exchange $500K property (basis $200K) for $500K property.
Deferred gain: $300K
New basis: $500K - $300K = $200K (carryover)

sections.title: Qualified Intermediary
sections.content:
**Why needed:**
Cannot receive cash directly.

**QI holds:**
• Sale proceeds from relinquished property
• Transfers to seller of replacement property

**QI cannot be:**
• Related party
• Agent (attorney, CPA, broker in last 2 years)

**Must use QI before closing on sale!**
Cannot fix afterward.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Only real property qualifies (post-TCJA)
Must identify replacement within 45 days
Must close within 180 days
Boot (cash/debt relief) is taxable
Basis in new property = FMV minus deferred gain
Must use qualified intermediary

knowledgeCheck.question: After the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA), which of the following properties would qualify for a like-kind exchange under Section 1031?
options: A delivery truck used in a business
A painting held as an investment
Commercial land held for investment
Inventory held for sale to customers
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Only real property qualifies for a like-kind exchange after the TCJA. Commercial land held for investment is considered real property. The other options are personal property or inventory, which are not eligible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the key change after TCJA: the type of property that qualifies.

reveal.question: What is the deadline to identify replacement properties in a like-kind exchange?
reveal.answer: The deadline to identify replacement properties is 45 days from the date the relinquished property is sold (Day 0).

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following scenarios satisfies the identification requirements for replacement properties in a Section 1031 exchange?
options: Identifying four properties, regardless of their combined fair market value.
Identifying five properties with a total fair market value of 180% of the relinquished property's value.
Identifying three properties, regardless of their combined fair market value.
Identifying any number of properties as long as at least one is eventually acquired.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Taxpayers can identify up to three properties of any value, or any number of properties as long as their total fair market value does not exceed 200% of the relinquished property's value. Identifying four properties exceeds the three-property rule, and five properties with a FMV of 180% would be acceptable if there were fewer properties.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the number of properties allowed and the 200% rule.

title: Installment Sales
description: Understand tax-deferred payment arrangements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Installment sales spread gain recognition over the payment period! This defers tax and can keep taxpayers in lower brackets. Know when this method applies.

sections.title: What is an Installment Sale?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Sale where at least one payment received after year of sale.

**Default Method:**
Applies automatically unless elected out.

**Effect:**
Proportionate gain recognized as payments received.

sections.title: Ineligible Property
content: Inventory (cannot use installment method)
Publicly traded stocks and securities
Dealer dispositions (ordinary course of business)
Depreciation recapture (all recognized in year 1)

sections.title: Gross Profit Ratio Calculation
sections.content:
**Gross Profit Ratio:**
Gross Profit ÷ Contract Price

**Where:**
Gross Profit = Selling Price - Adjusted Basis
Contract Price = Selling Price - Existing Mortgage Assumed (if any)

**Each Payment:**
Payment × Gross Profit Ratio = Taxable Gain

**Interest:** Reported separately as ordinary income.

sections.title: Example
sections.content:
**Facts:**
• Selling price: $100,000
• Adjusted basis: $40,000
• Down payment: $20,000
• Balance: $80,000 over 4 years

**Calculation:**
• Gross profit: $100,000 - $40,000 = $60,000
• Contract price: $100,000
• Gross profit ratio: 60%

**Year 1 gain:**
$20,000 × 60% = $12,000 taxable

sections.title: Related Party Rules
sections.content:
**If buyer is related party:**

If buyer resells within 2 years, seller accelerates remaining gain.

**Related parties:**
• Spouse, children, grandchildren, parents
• Controlled corporations/partnerships
• Trusts

**Exceptions:**
• Sale of marketable securities
• Involuntary conversion
• Sale after death of original seller

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: At least one payment after year of sale
Gain recognized proportionately as payments received
Gross profit ratio = Gross profit ÷ Contract price
Depreciation recapture recognized in year 1
Cannot use for inventory or publicly traded stock
Related party resale within 2 years accelerates gain

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following sales would NOT be eligible for installment sale treatment under Section 453?
options: A sale of land where the buyer makes annual payments over 5 years.
A sale of publicly traded stock where the proceeds are received in installments over 3 years.
A sale of a building where the buyer assumes an existing mortgage and makes annual payments over 10 years.
A sale of equipment used in a business where the payments are spread over 2 years.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Publicly traded stock is specifically excluded from installment sale treatment. Gains from sales of publicly traded stock must be recognized in the year of sale.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which types of property are ineligible for installment sale treatment.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating the Gross Profit Ratio in an installment sale?
reveal.answer:
Gross Profit Ratio = Gross Profit ÷ Contract Price

Where:
Gross Profit = Selling Price - Adjusted Basis
Contract Price = Selling Price - Existing Mortgage Assumed (if any)

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah sells land with a selling price of $200,000 and an adjusted basis of $120,000. The buyer assumes Sarah's existing mortgage of $50,000. In the first year, the buyer pays Sarah $30,000. What is the taxable gain Sarah must recognize in the first year?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the Gross Profit Ratio first, then multiply it by the payment received.

title: Self-Employment Tax
description: Understand SE tax calculation and payment

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Self-employed individuals pay BOTH employer and employee portions of Social Security and Medicare! Understanding SE tax helps clients budget and claim proper deductions.

sections.title: SE Tax Calculation
sections.content:
**Step 1:** Calculate net self-employment income

**Step 2:** Multiply by 92.35% (equivalent to employer deduction)

**Step 3:** Apply rates:
• 12.4% on first $168,600
• 2.9% on all SE income
• 0.9% additional Medicare on income over threshold

**Deduction:**
50% of SE tax is deductible (Schedule 1, Line 15).

sections.title: What Income is Subject to SE Tax?
sections.content:
**Subject to SE Tax:**
• Schedule C net profit
• General partner's share of partnership income
• Director fees (non-employee)
• Self-employment consulting income

**NOT Subject to SE Tax:**
• Limited partner distributive share (generally)
• S corporation distributions
• Rental income (generally)
• Dividends/interest
• Capital gains

sections.title: Additional Medicare Tax
sections.content:
**0.9% Additional Tax:**

Applies when SE income plus wages exceed:
• Single/HOH: $200,000
• MFJ: $250,000
• MFS: $125,000

**Not split between employer/employee** - all paid by taxpayer.

**No above-the-line deduction** for additional Medicare tax.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SE tax = 15.3% (12.4% SS + 2.9% Medicare)
Calculate on 92.35% of net SE income
50% of SE tax is above-the-line deduction
SS portion limited to wage base ($168,600 in 2024)
Additional 0.9% Medicare over $200K/$250K
S corp distributions avoid SE tax (with reasonable salary)

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, a self-employed consultant, has a net profit of $200,000 from her Schedule C business. What is the amount of her self-employment income subject to the 12.4% social security tax?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The social security portion of self-employment tax (12.4%) is only applied to the first $168,600 of self-employment income (for the tax year the lesson is based on). While Sarah's net profit is $200,000, the maximum amount subject to the 12.4% tax is $168,600.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the annual limit for Social Security tax.

reveal.question: What deduction can a self-employed individual take related to self-employment tax, and where is it taken?
reveal.answer: A self-employed individual can deduct one-half of their self-employment tax. This deduction is taken on Schedule 1 of Form 1040.

sections.title: Subject vs. Not Subject to SE Tax

rows.label: Income Type
values: Schedule C Net Profit
S Corporation Distributions

rows.label: Partnership Income
values: General Partner's Share
Limited Partner's Distributive Share (generally)

rows.label: Other
values: Director Fees (Non-Employee)
Rental Income (Generally)

title: Estimated Tax for Self-Employed
description: Understand estimated tax requirements for business owners

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Self-employed individuals must make quarterly estimated tax payments! No employer withholds for them. Understanding the rules prevents penalties.

sections.title: Who Must Pay?
sections.content:
**Required if expecting to owe $1,000+ when filing return:**

• Self-employed individuals
• Partners (for SE tax and income tax)
• S corporation shareholders (for distributions)
• Investors with significant income

**No requirement if:**
• Prior year tax = $0
• U.S. citizen/resident entire year
• Prior year was 12-month year

sections.title: Safe Harbor Rules
sections.content:
**No penalty if pay either:**

**Option 1:** 90% of current year tax

**Option 2:** 100% of prior year tax
• If prior year AGI > $150,000 ($75,000 MFS): 110%

**Most use prior year safe harbor** - it's predictable!

**Requirement:** Prior year was 12 months with tax liability.

sections.title: Payment Options
sections.content:
**How to Pay:**

• IRS Direct Pay (free, bank account)
• EFTPS (free, enrollment required)
• Credit/debit card (fees apply)
• Mail check with voucher (Form 1040-ES)

**Increase Withholding:**
If spouse works, increase W-4 withholding to cover estimate.

**Withholding treated as paid evenly through year!**

sections.title: Annualized Income Method
sections.content:
**For Seasonal/Variable Income:**

If income not evenly distributed, can annualize each quarter.

**Form 2210, Schedule AI** required.

**Benefit:** May reduce early quarter payments.

**Example:** Retail business makes 60% of income in Q4.
Can pay lower estimates in Q1-Q3, larger in Q4.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Required if expecting to owe $1,000+
Due: April 15, June 15, September 15, January 15
Safe harbor: 100% of prior year (110% if AGI > $150K)
Form 1040-ES for mailed payments
Can increase W-4 withholding instead
Annualized method for seasonal income

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is self-employed and projects her total tax liability for the current year will be $1,200. After subtracting her withholdings, she expects to owe $1,100 at the end of the year. Which of the following statements is correct regarding Sarah's estimated tax payments?
options: Sarah is not required to make estimated tax payments because her total tax liability is less than $2,000.
Sarah is required to make estimated tax payments because she expects to owe $1,000 or more when filing her return.
Sarah is only required to make estimated tax payments if her self-employment income exceeds $10,000.
Sarah is not required to make estimated tax payments if her adjusted gross income (AGI) is below a certain threshold.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The requirement to make estimated tax payments is triggered when an individual expects to owe $1,000 or more in taxes after subtracting withholdings and credits. Sarah expects to owe $1,100, so she meets this threshold.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the dollar amount that triggers the estimated tax payment requirement.

sections.title: Safe Harbor Rules: Prior Year AGI Threshold

reveal.question: What is the Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) threshold for using 110% of the prior year's tax liability as a safe harbor for estimated tax payments (for single filers)?
reveal.answer: $150,000. If your prior year AGI exceeded $150,000 (or $75,000 if Married Filing Separately), you must pay at least 110% of your prior year's tax liability to avoid an underpayment penalty. Otherwise, you can use 100%.

sections.title: Payment Methods: IRS Direct Pay vs. EFTPS

rows.label: Cost
values: Free
Free

rows.label: Account Requirement
values: Requires bank account information each time
Requires enrollment and PIN

rows.label: Best For
values: Occasional payments
Frequent payments and business taxes

rows.label: Convenience
values: Quick and easy for one-time use
Streamlined process after enrollment

knowledgeCheck.question: John is self-employed. His current year estimated tax liability is $10,000. His prior year tax liability was $8,000, and his prior year AGI was $160,000. What is the MINIMUM amount John must pay in estimated taxes this year to avoid an underpayment penalty, using the safe harbor method?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Because John's prior year AGI exceeded $150,000, he must use 110% of his prior year's tax liability as the safe harbor. 110% of $8,000 is $8,800. He does not need to pay 90% of his current year liability if his prior year calculation is lower.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the AGI threshold for the 110% rule.

title: Business Tax Compliance
description: Understand information returns and compliance requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Businesses must file information returns reporting payments to others! Miss a deadline or file incorrectly, and penalties add up fast. Know the requirements!

sections.title: 1099-NEC Requirements
sections.content:
**File if paying:**
• $600+ to non-employee for services
• Attorneys (any amount for legal services)

**Due Dates:**
• To recipient: January 31
• To IRS: January 31

**Must file for:**
• Independent contractors
• Self-employed individuals
• Partnerships (but not corporations generally)

sections.title: TIN/W-9 Requirements
sections.content:
**Request W-9 before payment:**

• Get payee's name and TIN (SSN or EIN)
• Certify not subject to backup withholding

**If payee refuses or provides incorrect TIN:**
• Backup withholding required: 24%
• Deduct from payments, deposit with IRS
• Report on Form 945

sections.title: Electronic Filing
sections.content:
**E-filing Required if:**
• 10 or more information returns (aggregate)

**Benefits:**
• Faster processing
• Fewer errors
• Extended correction period

**FIRE System:** IRS online filing for 1099s

**Third-party services available:** Many payroll and accounting services file automatically.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: 1099-NEC due January 31 to IRS and recipients
Get W-9 before making payments
Backup withhold 24% if no TIN provided
Penalties: $60-$630 per late/incorrect return
E-file required for 10+ returns
Corporations generally exempt from 1099 (except attorneys)

knowledgeCheck.question:
Which of the following scenarios requires filing a Form 1099-NEC?

A) Paying a corporation $1,000 for consulting services.
B) Paying an attorney $300 for legal services related to a business dispute.

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. A 1099-NEC must be filed for any amount paid to an attorney for legal services. A 1099-NEC is generally not required for payments to corporations (option A) or for amounts under $600 to non-employees for services (options C and D).

reveal.question: What percentage is the backup withholding rate, and what triggers the requirement to apply backup withholding?
reveal.answer: The backup withholding rate is 24%. It is triggered when a payee refuses to provide their Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN) or provides an incorrect TIN.

knowledgeCheck.question: On which form is backup withholding reported and paid to the IRS?
options: Form 941
Form 1040-ES
Form 945
Form 940
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Backup withholding is reported and paid to the IRS using Form 945, Annual Return of Withheld Federal Income Tax. Form 941 is used to report payroll taxes, Form 1040-ES is for estimated taxes for individuals, and Form 940 is for federal unemployment tax (FUTA).
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which form is specifically designed for reporting withheld income tax that isn't related to payroll.
